title: Neptune AIS Platform v3
description:
## Context
This root epic captures the full Neptune v3 package strategy: a local AIS lakehouse, a canonical dataset family, explicit provenance and QC, derived maritime analytics, streaming semantics, and release-readiness hardening.

## Why This Matters
Without a single root that anchors the architectural promises and backlog translation rules, the work can fragment into unrelated storage, adapter, and analytics efforts that no longer add up to the intended product.

## Scope
- Own the end-to-end program definition for storage, catalog, sources, fusion, tracks, events, streaming, ecosystem polish, and release readiness.
- Provide the top-level parent for all phase epics and capability epics created from the elaborated v3 plan.
- Preserve the architectural pillars that make Neptune a reproducible local maritime data platform rather than a thin download wrapper.

## Success Criteria
- The full phase hierarchy exists beneath this epic and is traceable back to the canonical `.plans` document.
- Cross-phase dependencies reflect the architectural sequencing described in the elaborated v3 plan.
- Future contributors can understand project intent, delivery order, and major non-goals from this epic and its descendants.

## Dependencies
- None; this is the program root for the Neptune v3 effort.

## Non-Goals
- Implementing code directly inside this epic.
- Encoding low-level API signatures that belong in child work.
- Replacing detailed design notes that belong in capability epics.
design: Architecture pillar owner. Keep storage reliability, provenance legibility, and product-level helper APIs in view when child work starts to optimize locally.
notes: Future-self note: if a new workstream does not obviously map back to one of the canonical datasets, execution engines, source integrations, or release hardening, challenge whether it belongs in Neptune v3.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: the full v3 plan, especially Product goals, Core design decisions, Implementation phases, and the execution appendix. Sequencing rationale: one root epic keeps the backlog aligned with the product instead of letting each subsystem evolve its own uncoordinated definition of success. Key consideration: Neptune should stay simple for notebook users while remaining rigorous about storage, provenance, and reproducibility.

title: Phase 0: Planning normalization and backlog substrate
description:
## Context
Phase 0 creates the canonical plan artifact and the self-documenting bead graph that future implementation work will execute against.

## Why This Matters
This work is foundational because the quality of the backlog determines whether future execution preserves the architecture or slowly erodes it.

## Scope
- Canonicalize the v3 plan under `.plans/`.
- Elaborate invariants, gates, risks, and cross-links needed for execution.
- Initialize `bd`, create the backlog hierarchy, validate dependencies, and backfill bead IDs into the plan.

## Success Criteria
- A canonical `.plans` file exists and is more detailed than the root snapshot.
- The bead graph is created, commented, dependency-wired, and free of cycles.
- The plan and backlog can be navigated from either direction using the backlog index.

## Dependencies
- The root v3 markdown snapshot.
- A writable repository workspace with `bd` available.

## Non-Goals
- Implementing Neptune runtime code.
- Assigning owners or deadlines.
- Claiming uncertain design choices are settled when they are intentionally deferred.
design: Planning and backlog substrate. Treat this as the one-time normalization layer that future work depends on.
notes: Future-self note: if the backlog diverges from the canonical plan, update both together so resumption stays cheap.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Elaboration Appendix / Phase 0. Sequencing rationale: every product phase depends on a coherent planning substrate, not because planning is sacred, but because Neptune’s architecture has enough moving parts that an underspecified backlog would create expensive drift.

title: P0: Canonical plan artifact and narrative elaboration
description:
## Context
This epic owns the canonical `.plans` artifact and the extra narrative detail needed to turn the v3 snapshot into an execution-grade specification.

## Why This Matters
The root markdown already captures the product direction; this epic makes it usable for implementation, resumption, and backlog translation.

## Scope
- Copy the root v3 plan into `.plans` as the canonical working artifact.
- Add architectural invariants, phase gates, deferred decisions, and detailed phase elaboration.
- Preserve the role of the root markdown as the original snapshot.

## Success Criteria
- The `.plans` file is the authoritative plan artifact.
- The execution appendix fills the earlier gaps around invariants, sequencing, and risk.
- The artifact roles of the root file and `.plans` file are documented clearly.

## Dependencies
- Phase 0 only; no product-phase blockers.

## Non-Goals
- Implementing runtime code.
- Changing the architectural intent of the original v3 plan without documenting why.
- Treating the appendix as a substitute for the earlier narrative sections.
design: Planning-artifact owner. Keep the appended material additive and execution-oriented so the canonical plan remains readable while becoming implementation-grade.
notes: Future-self note: if a future revision wants to change the plan substantially, preserve the rationale in both the document and the owning bead comment.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Elaboration appendix and the user request to make the v3 plan self-contained and self-documenting. Sequencing rationale: the `.plans` artifact must become canonical before the backlog can safely cite it. Consideration: avoid editing the root snapshot so there is always a clean point of comparison.

title: P0: Copy the root v3 plan into `.plans` as the canonical artifact
description:
## Context
The root markdown is the source snapshot, but execution needs a canonical working plan under `.plans`.

## Planned Work
- Create `.plans/Neptune AIS Package Plan v3.md` from the root snapshot.
- Keep the root file untouched so the original narrative remains available as a baseline.
- Make the `.plans` copy the artifact all later backlog references point at.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The `.plans` file exists and matches the root snapshot before elaboration begins.
- The root file remains untouched.
- Future work can cite the `.plans` path as canonical without ambiguity.

## Dependencies
- Root v3 markdown is present in the repository.

## Non-Goals
- Rewriting the plan during the copy step.
- Deleting the root file.
design: Artifact-copy task. Preserve source fidelity first, then elaborate.
notes: Future-self note: if the source snapshot ever changes, record that as a deliberate revision rather than silently overwriting the canonical `.plans` artifact.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: user instruction and Phase 0. Sequencing rationale: copy first so every later change lands in the canonical artifact. Consideration: this preserves a clean before/after comparison.

title: P0: Append execution-grade invariants, gates, and deferred decisions to the canonical plan
description:
## Context
The original v3 plan is architecturally strong but needed explicit invariants, gating rules, and deferred-decision handling for backlog generation.

## Planned Work
- Add architectural invariants for product shape, datasets, storage, execution, QC, provenance, and extensibility.
- Add phase execution rules, detailed phase elaboration, and risk/deferred-decision sections.
- Keep the additions clearly marked as an execution appendix rather than blending them invisibly into the original prose.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The canonical `.plans` file contains explicit invariants, phase gates, detailed phase elaboration, and a deferred-decision register.
- The appendix is readable as an operational companion to the original narrative.
- The additions preserve the intent of the root v3 plan while making execution safer.

## Dependencies
- `P0: Copy the root v3 plan into `.plans` as the canonical artifact`.

## Non-Goals
- Changing the fundamental goals of Neptune.
- Turning the plan into a low-level implementation manual.
design: Execution-appendix task. Add just enough structure for safe execution without burying the product narrative.
notes: Future-self note: appendices like this should clarify constraints and sequencing, not smuggle in unreviewed scope changes.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Elaboration Appendix requirements. Sequencing rationale: invariants and gates must be explicit before the backlog can encode them. Consideration: keep the appendix additive so future readers can still separate architecture from execution guidance.

title: P0: Document the roles of the root snapshot and canonical `.plans` artifact
description:
## Context
Future contributors should not have to infer which markdown file is authoritative for execution planning.

## Planned Work
- Explain that the root file is the original snapshot and the `.plans` copy is the canonical elaborated artifact.
- Capture why the split exists and how future revisions should be handled.
- Make the distinction visible in the plan and backlog narrative.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The canonical plan explicitly explains the role of both markdown artifacts.
- The backlog can reference the `.plans` file without ambiguity.
- A future contributor can update the planning artifact without accidentally overwriting the historical snapshot.

## Dependencies
- `P0: Append execution-grade invariants, gates, and deferred decisions to the canonical plan`.

## Non-Goals
- Maintaining multiple competing canonical plan files.
- Hiding the provenance of plan changes.
design: Documentation-only task that protects future resumption and auditability.
notes: Future-self note: if the root snapshot is ever retired, do it explicitly rather than by drift.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: user instruction to keep the root file untouched and use `.plans` as canonical. Sequencing rationale: the artifact split should be documented before the backlog points back to it. Consideration: ambiguity here would make every future planning discussion harder.

title: P0: Beads workspace and authoring taxonomy
description:
## Context
This epic defines how Neptune v3 work is represented in `bd`: issue types, labels, hierarchy, bodies, design notes, and future-self comments.

## Why This Matters
A self-documenting graph requires a stable authoring convention so that the backlog reads as a coherent system instead of a pile of tickets.

## Scope
- Initialize the repo-local SQLite `bd` workspace.
- Define labels, issue-type usage, and body/comment conventions.
- Record naming and hierarchy rules for root, phase, capability, and leaf beads.

## Success Criteria
- The `.beads` workspace exists and is repo-local.
- A consistent label and authoring taxonomy is used across the graph.
- Future beads can be added without inventing new conventions ad hoc.

## Dependencies
- Phase 0 only; no product-phase blockers.

## Non-Goals
- Switching to another tracker.
- Keeping bead structure implicit.
- Using bare titles with no architectural context.
design: Tracker-taxonomy owner. Optimize for resumability and graph legibility over minimal ticket text.
notes: Future-self note: if a new label or issue pattern is needed later, document why it was introduced so the taxonomy stays intentional.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: the user request for self-contained, self-documenting beads plus the execution appendix. Sequencing rationale: taxonomy comes early because every later issue should follow the same conventions. Consideration: keep the label set expressive but still small enough to scan quickly.

title: P0: Initialize the repo-local SQLite `bd` workspace
description:
## Context
The Neptune repo started without an existing authoritative beads database.

## Planned Work
- Initialize `.beads` with the default SQLite backend.
- Keep the workspace repo-local and non-stealth.
- Confirm the repo has a durable place to store the backlog graph.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `.beads` exists with a usable SQLite backend.
- The issue prefix is stable for Neptune work.
- Future `bd` commands can create, update, comment, and wire dependencies safely.

## Dependencies
- No blocker beyond Phase 0 workspace access.

## Non-Goals
- Using JSONL-only mode.
- Using stealth/local-only mode.
- Editing `.beads` files directly.
design: Workspace bootstrap task. Use the default SQLite mode to match the chosen execution plan.
notes: Future-self note: if the backend ever changes, treat it as a deliberate migration task rather than incidental tooling churn.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: execution plan assumptions. Sequencing rationale: the backlog cannot exist until the tracker workspace exists. Consideration: default SQLite is the most straightforward fit for this repo.

title: P0: Define label, issue-type, and comment conventions for Neptune v3
description:
## Context
The backlog needs a stable vocabulary for phases, areas, datasets, sources, and the meaning of epic versus task-level work.

## Planned Work
- Define the fixed label taxonomy used by the Neptune v3 graph.
- Standardize the use of epic, feature, task, and chore issue types.
- Standardize the expectation that every bead gets a future-self comment.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All beads in the graph use the agreed taxonomy.
- The meaning of labels and issue types is recoverable from the graph itself.
- Future additions can follow the same conventions without guesswork.

## Dependencies
- `P0: Initialize the repo-local SQLite `bd` workspace`.

## Non-Goals
- Creating one-off labels for every small topic.
- Leaving comment expectations implicit.
design: Convention-setting task. Favor stable reusable categories over exhaustive micro-labeling.
notes: Future-self note: when a new label appears later, check whether an existing label could have served the same purpose.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: authoring rules in the implementation plan. Sequencing rationale: conventions must exist before the issue hierarchy expands. Consideration: over-labeling is as harmful as under-labeling because it makes the graph noisy.

title: P0: Define hierarchy and naming rules for root, phase, capability, and leaf beads
description:
## Context
Neptune v3 needs a backlog shape that reveals both delivery order and subsystem ownership.

## Planned Work
- Document the hybrid hierarchy of root epic, phase epics, capability epics, and leaf tasks.
- Adopt phase-prefixed titles so the graph is readable in list and graph views.
- Tie hierarchy choices back to the architecture and sequencing rules in the canonical plan.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Issue titles and parentage reflect the agreed hierarchy.
- A future contributor can infer where a new bead should live in the tree.
- The graph can be navigated at multiple levels without losing context.

## Dependencies
- `P0: Define label, issue-type, and comment conventions for Neptune v3`.

## Non-Goals
- Using a flat backlog.
- Encoding sequencing only in titles and not in dependencies.
design: Hierarchy-definition task. Use titles that remain understandable in isolation, especially in graph and ready views.
notes: Future-self note: if a bead does not fit naturally into this hierarchy, question the decomposition before creating a new structural pattern.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: hybrid-spine backlog decision. Sequencing rationale: naming and hierarchy rules need to stabilize before capability and leaf beads are generated. Consideration: this is where readability and dependency truth meet.

title: P0: Graph validation and plan-to-backlog cross-linking
description:
## Context
This epic turns the created bead graph into a validated, navigable execution artifact by checking dependency health and backfilling plan references.

## Why This Matters
A large graph only helps if it is lint-clean, cycle-free, and tied back to the canonical plan so future readers can move between prose and execution safely.

## Scope
- Create the hierarchy and dependency graph in `bd`.
- Run graph health validation and readiness checks.
- Backfill actual bead IDs into the canonical plan and verify section coverage.

## Success Criteria
- The graph is free of dependency cycles.
- The plan contains a backlog index with real bead IDs.
- Coverage from major plan headings to beads has been checked explicitly.

## Dependencies
- `P0: Canonical plan artifact and narrative elaboration`.
- `P0: Beads workspace and authoring taxonomy`.

## Non-Goals
- Treating validation as optional cleanup.
- Leaving plan sections unmapped to work.
- Assuming the graph is healthy without checking it.
design: Validation-and-crosslink owner. This is the final trust-building layer for the backlog substrate.
notes: Future-self note: any time the graph changes materially, re-run the same validation posture instead of assuming old checks still cover the new shape.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: validation section of the implementation plan. Sequencing rationale: cross-linking and validation belong after the graph and taxonomy exist. Consideration: this is where the backlog becomes resumable rather than merely created.

title: P0: Create the full bead hierarchy and capture the dependency spine
description:
## Context
The canonical plan needs a concrete execution graph, not only prose about sequencing.

## Planned Work
- Create root, phase, capability, and leaf beads using the agreed hierarchy.
- Attach comments and design notes so each bead is self-describing.
- Add dependency edges that reflect phase gates and intra-phase sequencing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The Neptune v3 graph exists in `bd` with the intended hierarchy.
- Every bead has a comment and the required body sections.
- Dependencies reflect the execution truth described in the canonical plan.

## Dependencies
- `P0: Canonical plan artifact and narrative elaboration`.
- `P0: Beads workspace and authoring taxonomy`.

## Non-Goals
- Creating beads with empty bodies.
- Encoding dependencies only in prose.
design: Backlog-materialization task. The graph should preserve both sequencing and subsystem intent.
notes: Future-self note: if a future bulk update changes many beads, preserve the same level of comment and body quality rather than optimizing only for speed.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: the main user request. Sequencing rationale: once the plan and taxonomy are ready, the graph itself can be created. Consideration: this is the moment where architectural intent becomes executable work.

title: P0: Validate lint, cycle health, hierarchy shape, and ready-work behavior
description:
## Context
Graph creation is not enough; the resulting backlog has to be structurally healthy and readable through the standard `bd` views.

## Planned Work
- Run `bd lint` and correct any missing required sections.
- Run `bd dep cycles`, `bd list --tree`, `bd graph`, and `bd ready` against the root graph.
- Confirm the views reflect the intended hierarchy and starting-work semantics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Open epics and tasks satisfy `bd lint`.
- The graph is free of dependency cycles.
- Tree, graph, and ready views reflect the intended execution spine.

## Dependencies
- `P0: Create the full bead hierarchy and capture the dependency spine`.

## Non-Goals
- Skipping validation because the graph looks roughly correct.
- Treating a cycle-free graph as sufficient if the hierarchy is unreadable.
design: Validation task. Trust the standard `bd` views enough to shape the backlog for them.
notes: Future-self note: when later edits alter sequencing, rerun the same views to catch accidental regressions early.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Validation section of the plan. Sequencing rationale: validate only after the hierarchy and dependencies exist. Consideration: graph health is part of the deliverable, not a postscript.

title: P0: Backfill the canonical plan with backlog IDs and heading coverage notes
description:
## Context
The prose plan and the executable graph should remain mutually navigable so future readers do not have to search manually for the relevant work.

## Planned Work
- Replace placeholder IDs in the backlog index with the real root and phase bead IDs.
- Add a short section-to-bead mapping for the major implementation areas.
- Capture that heading coverage was checked across phases, API/CLI surfaces, source adapters, QC, and release work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The canonical `.plans` artifact contains actual bead IDs.
- A reader can move from a major plan area to the owning bead without searching the tracker manually.
- The coverage audit is explicitly recorded.

## Dependencies
- `P0: Validate lint, cycle health, hierarchy shape, and ready-work behavior`.

## Non-Goals
- Embedding every leaf bead ID in the markdown.
- Letting the plan and tracker drift apart after initial creation.
design: Cross-linking task. Keep the backlog index short but useful enough to orient a future maintainer immediately.
notes: Future-self note: if phase or capability titles change later, update the backlog index instead of treating it as historical trivia.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: plan appendix backlog-index requirements. Sequencing rationale: backfill only after IDs are stable and validation has passed. Consideration: a short accurate index is more valuable than a giant stale dump of every issue.

title: Phase 7: Release readiness
description:
## Context
Phase 7 certifies Neptune as a coherent releasable package through benchmarks, certification harnesses, migration/reproducibility coverage, packaging, and release-candidate checks.

## Why This Matters
Without explicit release-readiness work, the project risks shipping a technically impressive but operationally ambiguous prototype.

## Scope
- Benchmark the core paths.
- Certify adapters, migrations, and reproducibility behavior.
- Harden packaging, documentation, and release-candidate validation.

## Success Criteria
- Benchmark baselines exist and are explainable.
- Migration and rebuild behavior are tested intentionally.
- Packaging and documentation can support a public-facing release candidate.

## Dependencies
- Phases 2 through 6 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Indefinite polish loops.
- Major architecture rewrites during certification.
- Claiming guarantees the package does not yet provide.
design: Certification and release phase. Treat this as validation of the intended product, not a grab bag for late architecture discovery.
notes: Future-self note: release work surfaces debt that earlier phases tolerated; convert it into explicit backlog items rather than undocumented triage.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Key implementation notes, Dependencies, CLI, docs/examples intent, and Phase 7. Sequencing rationale: release readiness should validate the whole intended product surface, not only the archival core.

title: P7: Benchmarks, adapter certification, and reproducibility assurance
description:
## Context
This epic verifies that the product surface built across earlier phases is fast enough, adapter-consistent, and reproducible enough to support a real release.

## Why This Matters
Release confidence comes from proving that the core paths, adapters, and rebuild behavior work as intended under explicit checks.

## Scope
- Benchmark the core ingestion and query paths.
- Build the adapter certification harness.
- Add schema-migration and raw-rebuild reproducibility coverage.

## Success Criteria
- Benchmark baselines exist for core workflows.
- Adapter certification is systematic rather than ad hoc.
- Migration and rebuild behavior are tested explicitly.

## Dependencies
- Phases 2 through 6 complete.

## Non-Goals
- A final release checklist by itself.
- Pretending ad hoc manual checks are sufficient certification.
design: Release-certification epic. Treat performance, adapter consistency, and reproducibility as first-class release gates.
notes: Future-self note: the time to discover that rebuilds are not reproducible is before a release, not after users rely on the package.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Release readiness deliverables. Sequencing rationale: certification follows the completion of the major product surfaces it needs to verify.

title: P7: Establish performance benchmarks for ingestion, query, and track-oriented workflows
description:
## Context
Neptune needs explicit baseline numbers for the workflows it claims to support well.

## Planned Work
- Benchmark representative archival ingest paths.
- Benchmark representative query and track-oriented workloads.
- Record results in a form later releases can compare against.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Benchmarks exist for core ingestion and query paths.
- Results are structured enough for later comparison.
- Performance claims can be grounded in repeatable measurements.

## Dependencies
- Phases 2 through 6 complete.

## Non-Goals
- An exhaustive microbenchmark lab.
- Performance tuning every subsystem before baseline measurement exists.
design: Benchmark-baseline task for the release phase.
notes: Future-self note: a rough repeatable benchmark beats a perfect one that never gets maintained.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: performance benchmarks deliverable. Sequencing rationale: benchmark once the intended product surface is materially present.

title: P7: Build the adapter certification harness and readiness rubric
description:
## Context
As Neptune’s source list grows, release quality depends on certifying adapters consistently rather than case by case.

## Planned Work
- Define reusable adapter certification checks.
- Capture readiness criteria or rubric for archival, live, and community-style sources.
- Apply the harness to the current source set as part of release hardening.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A reusable adapter certification harness exists.
- Adapter readiness criteria are explicit.
- The current source set can be evaluated through one certification lens.

## Dependencies
- `P7: Establish performance benchmarks for ingestion, query, and track-oriented workflows`.

## Non-Goals
- A hosted certification service.
- Treating each adapter as a bespoke release story.
design: Certification-harness task for the expanding adapter ecosystem.
notes: Future-self note: the rubric matters because it tells you what “good enough to ship” means for very different source types.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: adapter test harness and source-expansion implications. Sequencing rationale: certification should build on benchmarked, already-implemented source paths.

title: P7: Add schema-migration and raw-rebuild reproducibility coverage
description:
## Context
Schema evolution and raw-retention replay are explicit parts of the Neptune architecture and need explicit release checks.

## Planned Work
- Add tests around schema or manifest migration behavior.
- Verify raw-retention rebuild reproducibility where the design promises it.
- Check that derived-cache invalidation behaves correctly across version changes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Migration behavior is explicitly tested.
- Raw-rebuild reproducibility is checked intentionally.
- Derived-cache invalidation across version changes is no longer a trust gap.

## Dependencies
- `P7: Build the adapter certification harness and readiness rubric`.

## Non-Goals
- A universal migration system for every hypothetical future change.
- Ignoring rebuild behavior because the current fixtures happen to work.
design: Migration-and-reproducibility task for release confidence.
notes: Future-self note: migration and rebuild trust are easy to assume and expensive to rediscover after release.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: schema migration tests, reproducibility tests, and derived cache invalidation notes. Sequencing rationale: this is the backbone of Neptune’s long-lived-cache story.

title: P7: Packaging, documentation audit, and release-candidate readiness
description:
## Context
This epic turns the validated package into a releasable artifact with coherent docs, packaging, and explicit release-candidate checks.

## Why This Matters
A technically sound package still needs packaging, docs, and final readiness gates to become a product users can adopt with confidence.

## Scope
- Harden release packaging and pipeline expectations.
- Audit docs and examples against the implemented product surface.
- Run an explicit release-candidate checklist.

## Success Criteria
- Release packaging is explicit and testable.
- Docs and examples accurately reflect the implemented platform.
- Release-candidate readiness is captured as an intentional checklist rather than informal confidence.

## Dependencies
- `P7: Benchmarks, adapter certification, and reproducibility assurance`.

## Non-Goals
- Infinite polish loops.
- Changing major architecture during release hardening.
design: Final release-readiness epic. This is where the product story, packaging, and certification results are made shippable together.
notes: Future-self note: if release readiness uncovers real gaps, turn them into explicit follow-up work instead of burying them inside the release narrative.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Release readiness phase and docs/examples intent. Sequencing rationale: packaging and final readiness should build on completed certification work rather than racing ahead of it.

title: P7: Harden packaging and release-pipeline expectations
description:
## Context
The release should be reproducible and packaging-aware rather than a manual best-effort exercise.

## Planned Work
- Review packaging outputs and release mechanics against the implemented dependency and extras model.
- Capture the release-pipeline steps or expectations needed for a real cut.
- Keep packaging aligned with the validated product surface.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Release packaging expectations are explicit.
- The release path is coherent with the package’s dependency and extras model.
- A real release cut is technically feasible from the current state.

## Dependencies
- `P7: Add schema-migration and raw-rebuild reproducibility coverage`.

## Non-Goals
- A full hosted release platform.
- Unreviewed release automation that obscures what is happening.
design: Packaging-and-release-mechanics task for the final phase.
notes: Future-self note: the release pipeline should explain the package, not hide it.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: packaging and release pipeline deliverables. Sequencing rationale: release mechanics should follow the certification work they package.

title: P7: Audit documentation and examples against the implemented Neptune surface
description:
## Context
Docs and examples should describe the Neptune that actually exists, not the Neptune people remember planning.

## Planned Work
- Audit docs and examples against the implemented API, CLI, source, and helper surfaces.
- Correct any drift introduced by earlier phase evolution.
- Preserve example accuracy as part of release readiness.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Docs and examples match the implemented product.
- Obvious drift between docs and code is removed.
- Release confidence includes documentation accuracy.

## Dependencies
- `P7: Harden packaging and release-pipeline expectations`.

## Non-Goals
- A rewrite of the whole documentation style.
- Leaving known doc drift for after release.
design: Accuracy-audit task for release docs and examples.
notes: Future-self note: stale docs create support debt faster than many code bugs.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: docs audit and examples expectations. Sequencing rationale: documentation should be audited after the release mechanics and validated product surface are known.

title: P7: Run the release-candidate checklist and record open follow-up work explicitly
description:
## Context
A release candidate should be an intentional state with explicit checks and explicit follow-up items rather than a feeling that things are probably okay.

## Planned Work
- Define and run a release-candidate checklist against the validated package.
- Record any remaining issues as explicit follow-up work.
- Close the Neptune v3 planning loop with a concrete readiness statement.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A release-candidate checklist exists and is run intentionally.
- Remaining release blockers or follow-ups are explicit.
- Phase 7 ends with a concrete readiness record.

## Dependencies
- `P7: Audit documentation and examples against the implemented Neptune surface`.

## Non-Goals
- Declaring release readiness from intuition alone.
- Hiding known follow-up work in a verbal summary.
design: Final readiness-check task for the Neptune v3 release story.
notes: Future-self note: a release candidate is only trustworthy when its open questions are explicit.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: release-candidate readiness checks. Sequencing rationale: the checklist should close the phase after packaging and docs have already been audited.

title: Phase 1: Core storage, catalog, and NOAA foundation
description:
## Context
Phase 1 establishes the archival core of Neptune: package structure, canonical schemas, manifest semantics, storage behavior, NOAA ingestion, QC foundations, and dual-engine access.

## Why This Matters
This phase proves Neptune can ingest, normalize, catalog, and query one trustworthy archival source with reproducible local semantics.

## Scope
- Build the package skeleton and core module boundaries.
- Define canonical contracts for positions, vessels, manifests, and schema versions.
- Implement storage, catalog, QC, NOAA adapter, unified API, DuckDB access, and baseline CLI/E2E flows.

## Success Criteria
- A user can ingest NOAA data into canonical partitions and inspect catalog, provenance, and QC results.
- Polars and DuckDB work over the same cataloged data.
- The Phase 1 path is strong enough to serve as the reference pattern for later adapters and derived datasets.

## Dependencies
- Phase 0 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Multi-source fusion beyond basic source awareness.
- Track, event, and streaming-grade features.
- Broad source ecosystem expansion.
design: Archival core foundation. Keep NOAA as the first reference adapter, not a special-case shortcut.
notes: Future-self note: every convenience taken in schema, manifest, or atomic-write behavior here will compound in later phases.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Local storage architecture, Data model, Query engine architecture, CLI, Package structure, Dependencies, Key implementation notes, and Phase 1. Sequencing rationale: Phase 1 is the minimum viable Neptune platform.

title: P1: Package topology and module seams
description:
## Context
This epic defines the package layout and module seams that later storage, adapter, derivation, and CLI work will live inside.

## Why This Matters
A clean package topology is the cheapest place to prevent boundary drift between archival ingestion, derived products, streaming, and user-facing surfaces.

## Scope
- Define the core package layout, entrypoints, and module responsibilities.
- Map the datasets, adapters, derive, geometry, and CLI areas into a coherent module tree.
- Record dependency-group expectations so optional geometry and streaming stacks stay at the edges.

## Success Criteria
- The module tree aligns with the v3 package-structure intent.
- Public entrypoints and internal seams are understandable before implementation expands.
- Optional dependencies are planned at the package boundary instead of leaking into the core.

## Dependencies
- Phase 0 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Implementing full runtime behavior for every module.
- Letting package structure emerge accidentally from the first few files.
design: Module-boundary owner. This epic should keep Neptune organized around stable concepts rather than around whichever source or feature arrives first.
notes: Future-self note: package seams are strategic; if a later task wants to cross them casually, revisit the architecture before merging code.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Package structure, Dependencies, and Phase 1. Sequencing rationale: package seams come before detailed contracts and implementations because they frame all later code placement.

title: P1: Establish package layout and public entrypoints
description:
## Context
Neptune needs a coherent top-level package shape before subsystem code accumulates.

## Planned Work
- Define `neptune_ais` public entrypoints and core modules.
- Reserve clear homes for catalog, storage, API, SQL, viz, and helper logic.
- Make the package structure legible to future contributors.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The planned module tree exists or is explicitly specified for implementation.
- Core public entrypoints are named and scoped intentionally.
- Subsystem owners know where related code belongs.

## Dependencies
- Phase 0 bootstrap complete.

## Non-Goals
- Filling every module with final logic.
- Bundling optional integrations into the root namespace.
design: Foundation task for code placement and public entrypoints.
notes: Future-self note: keep the top-level package narrow; most complexity belongs below stable subsystem boundaries.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Package structure section. Sequencing rationale: public entrypoints and module homes should not be left to chance. Consideration: narrow public entrypoints make compatibility management easier.

title: P1: Define subsystem boundaries for datasets, adapters, derivations, geometry, and CLI
description:
## Context
Neptune v3 spans archival, derived, and live capabilities, so internal subsystem seams need to be explicit early.

## Planned Work
- Define the role of `datasets`, `adapters`, `derive`, `geometry`, and `cli` packages.
- Document what should stay inside each subsystem and what should remain cross-cutting infrastructure.
- Prevent helper APIs or adapters from quietly absorbing storage or provenance logic.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Subsystem boundaries are explicit and consistent with the plan.
- Cross-cutting concerns such as storage and provenance are not trapped inside feature-specific packages.
- Later work can land in predictable places.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Establish package layout and public entrypoints`.

## Non-Goals
- Finalizing every internal class or function.
- Using subsystem names without explaining their ownership.
design: Subsystem-seam task. The goal is to keep architecture visible in the code layout.
notes: Future-self note: if a subsystem starts owning too many unrelated concerns, split its backlog before it turns into a junk drawer.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Package structure and implementation phases. Sequencing rationale: subsystem boundaries must be explicit before contracts and adapters fill them. Consideration: this is where Neptune avoids becoming a monolith.

title: P1: Baseline dependency groups and optional install boundaries
description:
## Context
The v3 plan intentionally keeps geometry and streaming dependencies optional.

## Planned Work
- Group core dependencies separately from optional analytics/geometry and streaming extras.
- Record which subsystems are allowed to import optional stacks directly.
- Preserve a lean core install path for archival and query workflows.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Core versus optional dependency groups are explicit.
- Optional geometry and streaming stacks are isolated at their intended edges.
- The package plan supports lean and full-feature installations without ambiguity.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Define subsystem boundaries for datasets, adapters, derivations, geometry, and CLI`.

## Non-Goals
- Pinning final package versions.
- Treating optional stacks as core requirements.
design: Dependency-boundary task. Keep optional capabilities additive rather than invasive.
notes: Future-self note: dependency sprawl often begins as one convenient import; resist that early.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Dependencies section and core design decisions. Sequencing rationale: dependency boundaries should be settled before implementation creates accidental coupling. Consideration: installation ergonomics are part of product quality.

title: P1: Canonical dataset and manifest contracts
description:
## Context
This epic defines the first version of the canonical schemas and the manifest contract that makes stored partitions legible and reproducible.

## Why This Matters
If the schemas and manifest shape are vague, later storage, QC, provenance, and source work will each invent their own incompatible assumptions.

## Scope
- Specify positions and vessels contracts for the Phase 1 archival core.
- Define manifest fields, version tokens, and compatibility visibility.
- Make schema evolution and mixed-version detection a first-class concern.

## Success Criteria
- Positions and vessels schemas are explicit enough for adapters and query engines to target consistently.
- The manifest contract records the metadata needed for reproducibility and partial-write detection.
- Versioning assumptions are visible before data lands on disk.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Package topology and module seams`.

## Non-Goals
- Finalizing every future dataset field for tracks or events.
- Treating schema evolution as a later concern.
design: Contract-defining epic. Keep the schema surface expressive but not bloated, and make manifests carry the operational truth.
notes: Future-self note: schema shortcuts taken here will surface later as fusion edge cases, migration pain, and provenance ambiguity.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Manifest schema, Data model, Key implementation notes, and Phase 1. Sequencing rationale: contracts come before storage and adapters so downstream work has a stable target.

title: P1: Specify the canonical `positions` schema v1
description:
## Context
`positions` is the most frequently queried canonical dataset and the foundation for tracks and events.

## Planned Work
- Define required and optional fields for canonical point observations.
- Preserve source, QC, confidence, and provenance fields in the contract.
- Record the semantics of timestamps, coordinates, identifiers, and nullable navigation fields.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The `positions` schema is explicit and implementation-ready.
- Required versus optional fields are documented intentionally.
- Source and QC lineage fields are part of the contract rather than afterthoughts.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Canonical dataset and manifest contracts`.

## Non-Goals
- Specifying track or event-only fields here.
- Silently dropping provenance or QC fields from the canonical surface.
design: Schema-definition task for the primary observation dataset.
notes: Future-self note: `positions` must remain rich enough for later derivation without becoming a single giant universal table.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Canonical dataset `positions`. Sequencing rationale: the primary canonical dataset should be settled before storage and adapter work lands. Consideration: provenance, QC, and confidence are part of the schema contract.

title: P1: Specify the canonical `vessels` schema v1
description:
## Context
`vessels` carries identity and slowly changing reference attributes that should not be repeatedly improvised inside point records or helpers.

## Planned Work
- Define the canonical vessel identity and attribute fields.
- Clarify how `vessels` relates to `positions` without collapsing them into one sparse table.
- Preserve source and lineage expectations for vessel records.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The `vessels` schema is explicit and aligned with the canonical dataset family.
- Identity and reference attributes have a clear home outside point observations.
- Source lineage remains visible in vessel records.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Specify the canonical `positions` schema v1`.

## Non-Goals
- Making `vessels` a full registry of every maritime entity.
- Using `vessels` to hide unresolved normalization choices in `positions`.
design: Reference-dataset schema task. Keep the contract small, linked, and useful to later helpers and fusion logic.
notes: Future-self note: resist moving every inconsistent field into `vessels`; keep the boundary purposeful.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Canonical dataset `vessels` and canonical dataset family rationale. Sequencing rationale: `vessels` should stabilize alongside `positions`, not after adapters already depend on ad hoc fields.

title: P1: Specify manifest fields, schema versions, and compatibility signals
description:
## Context
The local store only behaves like a trustworthy lakehouse if partitions are accompanied by explicit manifests and version metadata.

## Planned Work
- Define required manifest fields such as schema version, transform version, file lists, checksums, counts, timestamps, bbox, and QC counters.
- Specify how schema versions and mixed-version detection are represented.
- Ensure manifests can explain stale partitions and partial-write detection.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The manifest contract is explicit and ties storage to reproducibility.
- Version and compatibility signals are visible in the manifest design.
- Adapters and writers have a stable metadata target before implementation proceeds.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Specify the canonical `vessels` schema v1`.

## Non-Goals
- Choosing a final manifest serialization format if not yet required.
- Treating manifests as optional sidecars.
design: Operational metadata contract task. The manifest should tell the truth about what was written, from where, and under which assumptions.
notes: Future-self note: if a field seems annoying to record during writing, that is often a sign it will be essential during debugging.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Manifest schema and key implementation notes. Sequencing rationale: storage and adapters need a manifest target before they can write trustworthy partitions.

title: P1: Storage layout, retention policy, and atomic commit protocol
description:
## Context
This epic makes the local store deterministic, replayable, and safe against partial writes.

## Why This Matters
Neptune’s storage story is what turns a convenient cache into a reliable local data platform that can survive months of use and rebuilds.

## Scope
- Define partitioning, sort order, and file-layout rules.
- Formalize raw-retention behavior and source artifact tracking.
- Implement staged writes, validation, and commit markers.

## Success Criteria
- Canonical partitions follow deterministic layout rules.
- Raw retention and artifact tracking support replay and reproducibility.
- Half-written data cannot masquerade as a valid partition.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Canonical dataset and manifest contracts`.

## Non-Goals
- Complex performance tuning for every future source.
- Treating raw retention as always-on or always-off without policy.
design: Storage-semantics epic. Determinism and atomicity matter more than short-term implementation convenience.
notes: Future-self note: cache corruption bugs are often commit-protocol bugs in disguise; keep the write path explicit.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Local storage architecture, Raw retention policy, Partitioning and sort order, and Atomic writes. Sequencing rationale: adapters should not write anything until the layout and commit rules are explicit.

title: P1: Define partition layout, sharding, and sort-order rules for canonical data
description:
## Context
AIS workloads need layout decisions that reflect query patterns, not only date buckets.

## Planned Work
- Define dataset/source/date partition layout and optional intra-day sharding.
- Record `mmsi, timestamp` sort-order expectations and coarse spatial-key hooks.
- Make layout decisions explicit enough for both Polars scans and DuckDB access.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Canonical layout rules are deterministic and query-aware.
- Sort-order and partitioning assumptions are documented for writers and readers.
- The layout is compatible with later bbox- and source-aware pruning strategies.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Storage layout, retention policy, and atomic commit protocol`.

## Non-Goals
- Locking in every future optimization detail.
- Treating anonymous cache files as sufficient.
design: Layout-definition task. Optimize for long-lived correctness and reasonable pruning, not only easy first writes.
notes: Future-self note: layout choices become compatibility surfaces; change them deliberately.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Partitioning and sort order. Sequencing rationale: layout rules must exist before writers and readers can agree on the meaning of the local store.

title: P1: Define raw retention policy and source artifact tracking
description:
## Context
Reproducibility depends on more than canonical outputs; it also depends on knowing what raw artifacts produced them.

## Planned Work
- Define `raw_policy` modes and their intended behavior.
- Record artifact references, checksums, and key metadata needed for rebuilds.
- Make raw-retention expectations visible in manifests or related metadata.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Raw-retention modes are explicit and implementation-ready.
- Source artifacts can be traced back from canonical outputs.
- The rebuild story does not rely on upstream providers serving identical payloads forever.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Define partition layout, sharding, and sort-order rules for canonical data`.

## Non-Goals
- Storing every raw payload by default.
- Treating artifact provenance as optional metadata.
design: Retention-policy task. Preserve enough truth for replay without forcing one storage mode on all users.
notes: Future-self note: artifact tracking is often what makes a reproducibility bug fixable rather than mysterious.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Raw retention policy and provenance model. Sequencing rationale: artifact policy should be set before adapters decide what to keep or discard.

title: P1: Implement staged writes, validation gates, and commit markers for canonical partitions
description:
## Context
The local store needs write semantics that prevent partial or invalid partitions from looking complete.

## Planned Work
- Stage writes away from the final location.
- Validate schema and manifest expectations before committing.
- Record a commit or completion marker that distinguishes valid partitions from interrupted work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A partial write is detectable and does not look committed.
- Validation occurs before final partition visibility.
- Commit semantics are documented tightly enough for later derived and streaming promotion flows to reuse.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Define raw retention policy and source artifact tracking`.

## Non-Goals
- Optimizing away validation for convenience.
- Allowing half-written partitions into the catalog path.
design: Commit-protocol task. This is the trust anchor for all future storage operations.
notes: Future-self note: if later phases invent alternate write paths, they should inherit these guarantees instead of bypassing them.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Atomic writes and commit protocol. Sequencing rationale: reliable commit semantics must exist before adapters and catalogs can trust local data.

title: P1: Catalog, provenance, and QC foundations
description:
## Context
This epic builds the catalog surfaces, provenance visibility, and QC framework that make Neptune inspectable instead of opaque.

## Why This Matters
Users need to trust what Neptune stored, where it came from, and how questionable records were handled; otherwise the platform remains a black box.

## Scope
- Implement catalog and inventory surfaces.
- Expose provenance and version visibility at useful levels.
- Define QC classes, row-level flags, counters, and quality reports.

## Success Criteria
- Catalog and inventory surfaces can summarize stored datasets and partitions.
- Provenance and version information are visible enough for debugging and audit.
- QC is layered rather than binary and can be reported at row and dataset levels.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Storage layout, retention policy, and atomic commit protocol`.

## Non-Goals
- Building every future QC rule.
- Dropping all suspicious data automatically.
- Hiding provenance inside raw debug logs only.
design: Inspection-layer epic. The goal is not just to store data, but to make the stored data observable and trustworthy.
notes: Future-self note: if QC logic or provenance feels too verbose, remember that the cost of hidden data quality behavior is much higher later.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Cataloged local lakehouse, Data quality model, QC outputs, Provenance model, and Phase 1. Sequencing rationale: catalog and QC must mature before higher-level user access surfaces can be trusted.

title: P1: Implement catalog registry and inventory views for canonical datasets
description:
## Context
A local lakehouse needs discoverable metadata and inventory surfaces rather than a pile of path conventions.

## Planned Work
- Define dataset registration and version-ledger expectations.
- Expose inventory summaries for stored datasets and partitions.
- Support inventory views that later CLI and API surfaces can reuse.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Catalog registration expectations are explicit.
- Inventory views can summarize what is stored without manual path inspection.
- Later API and CLI work has a stable source of truth for dataset presence.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Implement staged writes, validation gates, and commit markers for canonical partitions`.

## Non-Goals
- A full external metadata service.
- Treating filenames alone as the catalog.
design: Catalog-registry task. Favor small reliable local truth over elaborate metadata orchestration.
notes: Future-self note: inventory views often become hidden product surfaces; keep them understandable and stable.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Catalog layer, inventory, and manifests sections. Sequencing rationale: inventory should build directly on deterministic storage semantics.

title: P1: Expose provenance, QC counters, and quality-report surfaces
description:
## Context
Catalog visibility is incomplete without provenance and QC summaries that tell the user how trustworthy stored data is.

## Planned Work
- Expose manifest- and dataset-level provenance summaries.
- Surface QC counters, severity summaries, and quality-report access points.
- Preserve enough detail to explain suspicious rows without requiring raw debugging first.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Provenance and QC summaries are available at meaningful inspection points.
- Quality-report behavior is explicit enough for API and CLI reuse.
- Users can tell whether anomalies were dropped, kept with flags, or normalized.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Implement catalog registry and inventory views for canonical datasets`.

## Non-Goals
- Pretending QC can be reduced to one global score.
- Hiding suspicious rows without explanation.
design: Inspection-reporting task. Keep provenance and QC visible at the right abstraction levels.
notes: Future-self note: the best QC surface is the one a user can inspect before they have to distrust the dataset.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: QC outputs and provenance model. Sequencing rationale: once catalog views exist, they should immediately gain provenance and quality context.

title: P1: Establish the hard-invalid versus suspicious QC framework
description:
## Context
The v3 plan intentionally avoids a drop-everything-that-looks-odd QC posture.

## Planned Work
- Define hard-invalid, suspicious, and source-quirk QC classes.
- Reserve row-level fields for flags, severity, and confidence.
- Make QC behavior explicit enough for NOAA and future adapters to plug into.

## Acceptance Criteria
- QC classes and row-level fields are explicit.
- Future adapters can reuse the same QC posture.
- The framework supports both strict invalid-row handling and annotated suspicious-row retention.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Expose provenance, QC counters, and quality-report surfaces`.

## Non-Goals
- Implementing every source-specific anomaly rule immediately.
- Conflating structural invalidity with suspicious-but-useful records.
design: QC-foundation task. The framework should be reusable and audit-friendly before it becomes large.
notes: Future-self note: once suspicious rows are silently dropped, provenance alone cannot recover the lost reasoning.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Data quality model and QC classes. Sequencing rationale: a stable QC framework should exist before NOAA normalization bakes in one-off assumptions.

title: P1: NOAA ingestion and user-facing access surfaces
description:
## Context
This epic turns the Phase 1 foundation into a usable product path by implementing NOAA ingestion, the unified `Neptune` object, DuckDB access, and baseline CLI flows.

## Why This Matters
Phase 1 is only complete when a user can actually fetch, catalog, and query NOAA data through the intended user-facing surfaces.

## Scope
- Implement the NOAA reference adapter.
- Expose the single-object `Neptune` access model plus DuckDB/SQL support.
- Add baseline CLI and end-to-end validation around the archival core.

## Success Criteria
- NOAA data can be fetched, normalized, stored, and queried through Neptune’s intended surfaces.
- Polars and DuckDB access share the same catalog truth.
- CLI and fixture-based acceptance checks prove the Phase 1 path end to end.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Catalog, provenance, and QC foundations`.

## Non-Goals
- Multiple-source behavior.
- Derived track/event features.
- Streaming support.
design: Reference-adapter and access-surface epic. Treat NOAA as the canonical first-path implementation of the Phase 1 architecture.
notes: Future-self note: if the NOAA path needs special logic, isolate it in the adapter rather than leaking it into shared storage or query interfaces.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Source adapters, Query engine architecture, Python API summary, CLI, and Phase 1. Sequencing rationale: NOAA and user-facing access surfaces should land only after contracts, storage, catalog, and QC foundations are ready.

title: P1: Implement the NOAA reference adapter and normalization flow
description:
## Context
NOAA is the first archival source and should exercise the common adapter contract rather than bypass it.

## Planned Work
- Fetch NOAA raw artifacts and record the raw-source lineage needed by retention policy.
- Normalize NOAA outputs into canonical `positions` and `vessels` shapes.
- Align NOAA handling with manifest, QC, and provenance expectations.

## Acceptance Criteria
- NOAA fetch and normalization behavior are explicit and aligned with shared contracts.
- NOAA outputs land as canonical data with traceable lineage.
- The adapter path is reusable as a pattern for later archival sources.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Establish the hard-invalid versus suspicious QC framework`.

## Non-Goals
- Hard-coding NOAA-specific shortcuts into shared code.
- Expanding into multi-source fusion behavior.
design: Reference-adapter implementation task. Preserve the common adapter shape even when NOAA specifics are convenient.
notes: Future-self note: NOAA is the first adapter, not the architecture.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: NOAA adapter references across the plan. Sequencing rationale: the first adapter should land only once shared contracts and QC surfaces exist. Consideration: this path becomes the template for DMA and later sources.

title: P1: Expose the unified `Neptune` object and DuckDB-backed query access
description:
## Context
The v3 plan replaces split dataset objects with one user-facing `Neptune` surface and first-class DuckDB support.

## Planned Work
- Implement the single-object constructor semantics for dates, sources, and cache behavior.
- Expose `positions`, `vessels`, inventory, provenance, quality-report, and DuckDB/SQL surfaces.
- Keep Polars lazy access primary while making DuckDB a first-class companion rather than an export escape hatch.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The `Neptune` API shape is explicit and aligned with the plan.
- DuckDB access operates over the same catalog as Polars access.
- User-facing accessors reflect the canonical dataset family rather than a bag of adapter-specific paths.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Implement the NOAA reference adapter and normalization flow`.

## Non-Goals
- Derived tracks/events helpers in Phase 1.
- Using separate single-date and multi-date objects again.
design: User-access-surface task. Preserve one mental model for users while keeping execution-engine choice flexible underneath.
notes: Future-self note: if an API addition only makes sense for one source or one engine, question whether it belongs on `Neptune` at all.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Unified dataset object, Core accessors, and Phase 1 deliverables. Sequencing rationale: user access should ride on top of a real reference ingestion path, not precede it.

title: P1: Add baseline CLI workflows and end-to-end archival validation
description:
## Context
Phase 1 should be usable outside notebooks and testable through an explicit end-to-end scenario.

## Planned Work
- Implement baseline CLI flows for download/refresh, inventory/manifests/qc inspection, and SQL/export.
- Create fixture-driven or smoke-style end-to-end coverage for the NOAA archival path.
- Capture Phase 1 acceptance as a repeatable validation story.

## Acceptance Criteria
- CLI flows exist for the core Phase 1 operations.
- An end-to-end archival scenario proves the NOAA path from fetch to query.
- Phase 1 acceptance is more than a narrative claim.

## Dependencies
- `P1: Expose the unified `Neptune` object and DuckDB-backed query access`.

## Non-Goals
- Every future CLI subcommand.
- Full release-grade benchmark coverage.
design: CLI-and-validation task. Use the CLI to prove the product path is coherent beyond direct library use.
notes: Future-self note: end-to-end checks are the easiest place to catch drift between storage, catalog, API, and CLI assumptions.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: CLI and Phase 1 validation expectations. Sequencing rationale: CLI and E2E coverage should validate the completed archival core rather than arrive before it.

title: Phase 2: Second archival source and explicit fusion
description:
## Context
Phase 2 proves Neptune is truly multi-source by adding DMA and formalizing fusion, deduplication, precedence, and lineage behavior.

## Why This Matters
Neptune only becomes a unified maritime product when multiple sources can be combined with explicit, inspectable rules rather than hidden fallback behavior.

## Scope
- Add DMA as the second archival source.
- Define source capability metadata and fusion configuration surfaces.
- Implement merge policies, dedup/provenance logic, and fused reporting/regression coverage.

## Success Criteria
- Users can query multiple sources with explicit merge modes.
- Fused outputs explain which source won and why.
- Regression coverage exists for multi-source behavior and source capability reporting.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Derived events and helper APIs that depend on stable fusion semantics.
- Broad adapter expansion.
- Streaming concerns beyond what capability metadata must describe.
design: Fusion foundation. Keep the distinction between source capability metadata, merge policy, dedup logic, and reporting legible.
notes: Future-self note: fusion bugs are often provenance bugs in disguise, so preserve row-level lineage even if it feels verbose.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Explicit multi-source fusion, Multi-source fusion model, Source adapters, Data sources, Python API summary, and Phase 2. Sequencing rationale: Neptune should prove a second archival path before building product features that assume fused data is trustworthy.

title: P2: DMA adapter and source-capability foundation
description:
## Context
This epic adds the second archival source and formalizes the self-describing capability metadata that later discovery and fusion rely on.

## Why This Matters
Neptune cannot claim real multi-source support until a second archival source exercises the adapter contract and exposes comparable capability metadata.

## Scope
- Implement DMA as the second archival source.
- Record capability metadata relevant to backfill, streaming, bbox support, incremental behavior, auth, and latency expectations.
- Add fixtures and conformance coverage for the new source.

## Success Criteria
- DMA is integrated as a proper archival adapter.
- Capability metadata exists as part of the source model rather than hidden implementation detail.
- The second source path validates the shared adapter contract.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Full fusion logic by itself.
- Broad source expansion beyond DMA.
design: Second-source foundation. Keep adapter behavior and source metadata explicit enough to reuse in later discovery and plugin surfaces.
notes: Future-self note: treat capability metadata as user-facing truth, not just adapter-internal config.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Source adapters, Data sources, and Phase 2. Sequencing rationale: the second source should prove the adapter contract and capability model before fusion becomes complex.

title: P2: Implement DMA fetch, normalization, and archival-source conformance
description:
## Context
DMA is the planned second archival source and should extend the shared adapter path without special cases.

## Planned Work
- Implement DMA fetch and raw-artifact handling.
- Normalize DMA outputs into canonical shapes compatible with Phase 1 contracts.
- Capture the key fixture and conformance expectations for the second archival path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- DMA behaves like a real second archival adapter, not a one-off import script.
- DMA normalization targets the canonical contracts cleanly.
- Fixture/conformance expectations exist for regression use.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Multi-source merge logic.
- Broad source-discovery UX.
design: Adapter implementation task for the Phase 2 reference second source.
notes: Future-self note: the second adapter is where shared assumptions are tested; keep them visible.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: DMA references in the v3 plan. Sequencing rationale: DMA lands first so fusion has two real sources to reason about.

title: P2: Define source capability metadata and programmatic capability reporting
description:
## Context
Source differences should be visible in a first-class capability model rather than inferred from docs or adapter code.

## Planned Work
- Define capability fields such as backfill, streaming, bbox, incremental, auth, latency, and licensing notes.
- Expose capability reporting through source metadata surfaces.
- Make capability metadata reusable for later source-discovery and plugin work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Capability metadata is explicit and queryable.
- DMA and NOAA can be described through the same capability model.
- Later discovery and CLI work has a stable metadata source.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Implement DMA fetch, normalization, and archival-source conformance`.

## Non-Goals
- Designing the final CLI UX for every source.
- Hiding source constraints inside docs only.
design: Capability-model task. This becomes the metadata layer for source discovery and fusion explainability.
notes: Future-self note: source metadata earns its keep when users can inspect it before running a workflow.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Source adapter interface and source catalog capabilities. Sequencing rationale: capability metadata should stabilize alongside the second source, not much later.

title: P2: Add second-source fixtures and compatibility checks for DMA
description:
## Context
A second archival source only improves confidence if it also improves the test matrix.

## Planned Work
- Add DMA raw and normalized fixtures that exercise the shared contract.
- Check schema conformance and provenance expectations against the new source.
- Preserve these fixtures as the baseline for future fusion tests.

## Acceptance Criteria
- DMA fixtures exist for raw, normalized, and conformance scenarios.
- Schema and provenance expectations are testable for the second source.
- Phase 2 fusion work can build on real multi-source fixtures.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Define source capability metadata and programmatic capability reporting`.

## Non-Goals
- Fusion correctness tests yet.
- Broad source-matrix certification.
design: Fixture-coverage task for the second archival source.
notes: Future-self note: these fixtures become the substrate for later fusion and source-expansion regression tests.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Adapter testing expectations. Sequencing rationale: the second-source fixture set should exist before fusion complexity is added.

title: P2: Fusion policy surface and merge-mode semantics
description:
## Context
This epic defines how users request multi-source results and what the supported merge modes actually mean.

## Why This Matters
Fusion is a product feature only if users can choose behavior explicitly and understand the implications of that choice.

## Scope
- Define merge policy configuration and validation.
- Implement `union`, `best`, and `prefer:<source>` semantics.
- Clarify where precedence, tolerance, and field selection behavior belong.

## Success Criteria
- Fusion modes are explicit, validated, and documented.
- Union, best, and preferred-source semantics are distinct and inspectable.
- Future fusion logic has a stable public contract.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- All dedup heuristics and confidence weighting details by itself.
- Source-discovery UX.
design: Policy-surface epic. Keep merge-mode semantics small, explicit, and explainable.
notes: Future-self note: merge policy is user-facing behavior, not just an internal switch.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Explicit multi-source fusion and Multi-source fusion model. Sequencing rationale: define the user-visible contract before deeper dedup and lineage machinery hardens around it.

title: P2: Define merge-policy configuration, validation, and tolerance inputs
description:
## Context
The user-facing fusion contract needs structured inputs for merge mode, precedence, and near-duplicate handling.

## Planned Work
- Define configuration inputs for merge mode, source preference, and tolerance settings.
- Validate supported values and reject ambiguous combinations.
- Preserve room for later extension without overcomplicating the first public contract.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fusion configuration is explicit and validation-ready.
- Invalid or ambiguous merge requests are rejectable.
- Later fusion work can depend on a stable configuration shape.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Implementing all merge behavior yet.
- Encoding per-source edge cases directly into the public API.
design: Public-contract task for multi-source selection behavior.
notes: Future-self note: every ambiguous input accepted now becomes a compatibility problem later.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Multi-source fusion model. Sequencing rationale: configuration and validation should exist before semantics are implemented.

title: P2: Implement `union` and `best` merge semantics over multi-source requests
description:
## Context
The default fusion path needs at least two distinct behaviors: union for completeness and best for deduplicated preference-aware results.

## Planned Work
- Implement `union` behavior that preserves contributing records.
- Implement `best` behavior that selects one preferred record per logical observation.
- Keep both behaviors provenance-aware and consistent with the configuration contract.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `union` and `best` semantics are distinct and testable.
- Both modes preserve enough provenance for later explanation.
- The implementation respects the earlier configuration and validation contract.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Define merge-policy configuration, validation, and tolerance inputs`.

## Non-Goals
- Preferred-source shorthand yet.
- All field-level conflict rules.
design: Merge-engine task for the two foundational fusion modes.
notes: Future-self note: if `best` cannot explain a chosen winner, the merge behavior is incomplete.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Explicit fusion behavior in the v3 plan. Sequencing rationale: get the foundational modes correct before adding preferred-source shorthand and richer precedence detail.

title: P2: Implement `prefer:<source>` semantics and source-order selection behavior
description:
## Context
Users need a deterministic way to bias fusion toward one source when that reflects their trust or operational context.

## Planned Work
- Add `prefer:<source>` behavior on top of the merge-policy contract.
- Make source-order selection explicit and inspectable.
- Preserve compatibility with provenance and later field-level precedence logic.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Preferred-source behavior is explicit and deterministic.
- The selected source can be explained in provenance output.
- Preferred-source behavior composes with the earlier merge modes cleanly.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Implement `union` and `best` merge semantics over multi-source requests`.

## Non-Goals
- A hidden priority-order fallback.
- Per-field precedence weighting by itself.
design: Source-preference task. This should stay deterministic and explainable rather than magical.
notes: Future-self note: explicit preference beats hidden defaults because users can reason about it.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: `merge="prefer:noaa"` and related fusion policy guidance. Sequencing rationale: preferred-source behavior should extend the foundational merge modes rather than replace them.

title: P2: Deduplication, precedence, and fused-row lineage
description:
## Context
This epic handles the record-level mechanics that make fusion both useful and auditable: dedup keys, tolerance windows, field precedence, confidence weighting, and row lineage.

## Why This Matters
Fusion fails as a product feature if it cannot both reduce duplicate noise and explain how a result was assembled.

## Scope
- Define dedup key and tolerance strategy.
- Handle field-level precedence and confidence weighting.
- Persist row-level fused provenance tokens and QC summaries.

## Success Criteria
- Near-duplicate logic is explicit enough to test and tune.
- Field-level conflict handling is deterministic.
- Fused rows remain explainable through provenance and QC outputs.

## Dependencies
- `P2: DMA adapter and source-capability foundation`.
- `P2: Fusion policy surface and merge-mode semantics`.

## Non-Goals
- Broad event-level reasoning.
- Hiding uncertainty behind one opaque winner-selection path.
design: Record-level fusion epic. Keep the dedup and lineage model inspectable enough that users can understand or challenge it.
notes: Future-self note: the fastest way to lose trust in fusion is to hide why records were merged or discarded.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Dedup key selection, field-level precedence, provenance tagging, and confidence-weight language in the v3 plan. Sequencing rationale: record-level mechanics should build on stable source and policy surfaces.

title: P2: Define dedup keys, timestamp tolerance, and near-duplicate matching rules
description:
## Context
Two sources can describe nearly the same logical observation without sharing an identical source record ID.

## Planned Work
- Define logical dedup keys across MMSI, time windows, coordinates, and source record hints.
- Specify timestamp tolerance and approximate spatial matching behavior.
- Make the rules testable rather than hidden inside implementation branches.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Dedup-key logic is explicit and configurable enough for the planned fusion modes.
- Near-duplicate behavior can be tested against fixtures.
- The dedup model is explainable to future maintainers.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Implement `prefer:<source>` semantics and source-order selection behavior`.

## Non-Goals
- Source-specific hacks that bypass the shared dedup model.
- An attempt to make every edge case disappear.
design: Dedup-contract task. The goal is controlled approximation, not fake precision.
notes: Future-self note: every heuristic here should be something you can describe in a comment or test name.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Dedup key examples in the Multi-source fusion model. Sequencing rationale: near-duplicate logic should be explicit before field precedence or fused QC depends on it.

title: P2: Define field-level precedence and confidence-weight handling for fused records
description:
## Context
Even after record matching, sources can disagree on specific fields such as ship type, destination, or quality indicators.

## Planned Work
- Define how field-level conflicts are resolved.
- Reserve a place for per-source confidence weighting where appropriate.
- Keep precedence decisions visible enough for provenance and QC reporting.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Field-level conflict handling is deterministic and documented.
- Confidence weighting, if used, has an explicit role.
- The fusion path does not silently discard disagreement.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Define dedup keys, timestamp tolerance, and near-duplicate matching rules`.

## Non-Goals
- A black-box scoring system with no inspectable rationale.
- Field precedence that differs by caller with no provenance trail.
design: Conflict-resolution task for fused rows. Make deterministic tradeoffs visible.
notes: Future-self note: field precedence is the place where “best” becomes political; keep the rules explicit.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: field-level precedence and optional per-source confidence weights. Sequencing rationale: conflict resolution should build on explicit dedup matching rather than guess at it.

title: P2: Persist fused-row provenance tokens, QC summaries, and winner explanations
description:
## Context
A fused row should still be traceable back to the contributing sources and the logic that selected the visible record.

## Planned Work
- Define row-level lineage tokens for fused outputs.
- Surface winner/contributor explanations and fused QC summaries.
- Preserve enough metadata for later inventory, helper, and debugging surfaces.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fused outputs retain row-level lineage or equivalent provenance tokens.
- Winner and contributor information is recoverable.
- Fused QC summaries can explain suspicious or lossy outcomes.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Define field-level precedence and confidence-weight handling for fused records`.

## Non-Goals
- A perfect explanation UI in Phase 2.
- Throwing away contributor context after selecting a winner.
