design: Lineage-preservation task. This is where fusion remains auditable instead of becoming magical.
notes: Future-self note: if fused provenance is missing, the rest of the platform cannot compensate for that loss later.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: provenance-tagging and QC concerns in multi-source fusion. Sequencing rationale: lineage should be preserved as the final step of record-level fusion logic.

title: P2: Multi-source reporting, accessors, and regression coverage
description:
## Context
This epic exposes the fused multi-source behavior through user-facing accessors and regression checks that prove the Phase 2 story end to end.

## Why This Matters
Fusion only becomes a real platform feature when users can inspect it through the same API/CLI surfaces they use for the single-source path.

## Scope
- Expose multi-source inventory, provenance, and quality-report surfaces.
- Add CLI or source-inspection affordances that explain fusion outcomes.
- Add regression coverage for the main Phase 2 semantics.

## Success Criteria
- Multi-source API and CLI surfaces make fusion inspectable.
- Regression coverage exists for merge modes, dedup, and provenance behavior.
- Phase 2 can be treated as a stable platform milestone.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Deduplication, precedence, and fused-row lineage`.

## Non-Goals
- Events or helper APIs that belong to later phases.
- Broad source-ecosystem documentation.
design: Reporting-and-regression epic. Prove that fusion is visible and repeatable, not merely implemented.
notes: Future-self note: good fusion regression tests are often the clearest documentation of what “best” or “prefer” really means.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: inventory, provenance, QC, Python API summary, CLI, and Phase 2. Sequencing rationale: user-facing reporting and regression coverage should validate the fully shaped fusion model rather than lead it.

title: P2: Expose multi-source inventory, provenance, and quality-report accessors
description:
## Context
Users need the same level of inspection for fused data that they already have for single-source archival data.

## Planned Work
- Extend inventory and provenance accessors to explain multi-source results.
- Surface quality-report behavior that reflects fused QC summaries.
- Keep the accessors aligned with the unified `Neptune` object rather than inventing special fusion-only side paths.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Users can inspect multi-source inventory, provenance, and quality behavior through stable accessors.
- The multi-source surfaces align with the existing API shape.
- Fusion results are explainable without dropping to raw manifests immediately.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Persist fused-row provenance tokens, QC summaries, and winner explanations`.

## Non-Goals
- Event-level helper APIs.
- GUI-style explanation tooling.
design: Access-surface task for multi-source explainability.
notes: Future-self note: if a user cannot inspect fused results from the main API, fusion will still feel like an opaque internal feature.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: inventory, provenance, and quality-report surfaces in the v3 plan. Sequencing rationale: accessors should sit on top of completed fusion lineage and QC behavior.

title: P2: Extend CLI and source-inspection workflows for fusion outcomes
description:
## Context
The CLI should be able to surface the same multi-source behavior and explanation cues as the library path.

## Planned Work
- Add CLI support for multi-source requests and merge-mode selection.
- Expose source inspection or provenance-oriented CLI views that explain contributors and winners.
- Keep the CLI aligned with the earlier source capability model.

## Acceptance Criteria
- CLI flows can request and inspect fused multi-source results.
- CLI output reflects merge mode and provenance behavior rather than hiding it.
- Source capability and fusion reporting are not library-only features.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Expose multi-source inventory, provenance, and quality-report accessors`.

## Non-Goals
- A full interactive TUI.
- Broad docs polish beyond the scoped Phase 2 CLI behavior.
design: CLI-surface task for Phase 2 multi-source features.
notes: Future-self note: CLI output is often where hidden semantics become obvious; use it to check whether the model is truly legible.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: CLI and source-capability reporting expectations. Sequencing rationale: CLI work should follow the stabilized multi-source accessor and provenance surfaces.

title: P2: Add regression and golden tests for merge modes, dedup, and provenance
description:
## Context
Fusion behavior is subtle enough that it needs fixtures and regression coverage to remain trustworthy over time.

## Planned Work
- Create golden scenarios for union, best, and preferred-source behavior.
- Add regression cases around dedup tolerance, field precedence, and fused lineage.
- Capture the expected behavior clearly enough that later source additions can reuse the same patterns.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fusion semantics are backed by explicit regression coverage.
- Dedup and provenance behavior are testable rather than hand-verified.
- Later phases can treat Phase 2 as a stable base instead of a moving target.

## Dependencies
- `P2: Extend CLI and source-inspection workflows for fusion outcomes`.

## Non-Goals
- Full release-readiness certification.
- Every future source combination.
design: Regression-coverage task for the fusion milestone.
notes: Future-self note: the best time to write fusion fixtures is while the intended behavior is still fresh and explicit.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Adapter testing and Phase 2 deliverables. Sequencing rationale: regression tests should validate the finished multi-source path, not a partial implementation.

title: Phase 3: Tracks and mapping foundations
description:
## Context
Phase 3 turns point observations into track products and map-ready layers using native columnar execution rather than eager GeoDataFrame bottlenecks.

## Why This Matters
Tracks and map-ready layers are how Neptune begins answering movement questions directly, which is part of the package’s product value rather than a downstream afterthought.

## Scope
- Define track schema, derivation config, cache rules, segmentation, aggregation, geometry emission, and map-layer foundations.
- Support positions, tracks, trips, and density-ready visualization primitives.
- Validate track derivation and map outputs with focused fixtures and acceptance checks.

## Success Criteria
- Tracks are queryable as a first-class dataset.
- Map workflows use derived/map-ready products instead of ad hoc eager conversions.
- The track pipeline is stable enough to support later event derivation and visualization.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Full event suite.
- Streaming promotion.
- Making heavy geometry dependencies mandatory in the core path.
design: Derived movement foundation. Keep geometry optional and derived cache invalidation explicit.
notes: Future-self note: if track identifiers or invalidation keys are unstable, every downstream event and map feature becomes fragile.
close_reason: Phase 3 complete. All 3 child epics closed: track schema/cache (4.1), native segmentation/materialization (4.2), geometry bridges/viz layers/validation (4.3). 115 tests passing, all success criteria met.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Track derivation architecture, Visualization architecture, Python API summary, CLI, and Phase 3. Sequencing rationale: event inference should build on a stable derived movement layer rather than raw points alone.

title: P3: Track schema, configuration, and cache contract
description:
## Context
This epic defines what a track is in Neptune, how users configure derivation, and what governs cache identity and invalidation.

## Why This Matters
Tracks become a durable product feature only when their schema and derivation inputs are explicit enough to cache and query safely.

## Scope
- Define the canonical track schema and stable track identifier expectations.
- Define the derivation configuration surface.
- Define cache keys and invalidation inputs for derived tracks.

## Success Criteria
- Track outputs have a clear schema and identity contract.
- Users or internal callers have explicit derivation-config inputs.
- Track cache invalidation is tied to the right upstream and configuration changes.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Implementing the full segmentation engine yet.
- Event-level semantics.
design: Derived-dataset contract epic. Keep track identity and cache behavior explicit before optimization concerns multiply.
notes: Future-self note: once track IDs and cache keys drift, downstream event logic becomes difficult to trust.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Canonical dataset `tracks`, Track derivation architecture, and Phase 3. Sequencing rationale: the derived dataset contract needs to exist before segmentation and map layers can rely on it.

title: P3: Define the canonical `tracks` schema and stable `track_id` rules
description:
## Context
Track outputs should be first-class and queryable, not a transient aggregation format.

## Planned Work
- Define the canonical track fields, including timing, counts, distance, bbox, and optional geometry/offset data.
- Clarify what makes a `track_id` stable enough for caching and downstream references.
- Preserve source and provenance expectations in the schema.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The `tracks` schema is explicit and implementation-ready.
- `track_id` semantics are stable enough for caching and downstream use.
- Track provenance is part of the contract.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Every future event-oriented extension field.
- A fully dynamic schema that changes per use case.
design: Schema-definition task for the derived track dataset.
notes: Future-self note: the more implicit `track_id` becomes, the harder it is to reason about cache validity and downstream references.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Canonical dataset `tracks`. Sequencing rationale: the derived output contract should be settled before segmentation code starts shaping it implicitly.

title: P3: Define the track-derivation configuration surface
description:
## Context
Track derivation has meaningful parameters such as gap thresholds, minimum points, and geometry inclusion.

## Planned Work
- Define the user-facing and internal configuration fields for track derivation.
- Make configuration values explicit enough to influence caching and reproducibility.
- Keep the default surface practical for notebook users while still precise enough for advanced use.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Track configuration is explicit and aligned with the plan examples.
- Configuration inputs can participate in cache identity.
- Defaults and advanced options can coexist without hidden behavior.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Define the canonical `tracks` schema and stable `track_id` rules`.

## Non-Goals
- Event-heuristic configuration.
- Unbounded custom logic injection into Phase 3.
design: Configuration-contract task for derived tracks.
notes: Future-self note: if a configuration knob changes results materially, it belongs in the cache identity and provenance story.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Track derivation configuration examples. Sequencing rationale: configuration should be explicit before segmentation code bakes in hidden defaults.

title: P3: Define track cache keys and invalidation triggers
description:
## Context
Derived tracks should be cacheable, but only if invalidation is tied to upstream data and derivation inputs.

## Planned Work
- Specify which upstream manifests, schema versions, and derivation config values invalidate track caches.
- Make invalidation visible in the track-manifest or equivalent metadata model.
- Keep the rules reusable for later event and map-derived caches.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Track cache invalidation inputs are explicit.
- Cache identity includes the upstream and config changes that matter.
- The Phase 3 design can support future derived-dataset caching.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Define the track-derivation configuration surface`.

## Non-Goals
- Generic caching for every future derived product.
- Pretending cached tracks are valid across silent config changes.
design: Cache-contract task for derived track data.
notes: Future-self note: cache invalidation rules deserve the same seriousness as schemas because they control whether users see truth or stale artifacts.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Derived dataset caching and Track derivation architecture. Sequencing rationale: invalidation should be explicit before the engine writes reusable track outputs.

title: P3: Native segmentation, aggregation, and track dataset materialization
description:
## Context
This epic implements the core native track-building pipeline over canonical positions.

## Why This Matters
The whole point of the Phase 3 shift is to avoid making pandas or GeoPandas the default bottleneck for large AIS movement analysis.

## Scope
- Implement boundary detection and segmentation.
- Aggregate track-level statistics and optional geometry/offset fields.
- Write and read track outputs as a proper derived dataset.

## Success Criteria
- Track segmentation works over the canonical positions path.
- Track outputs carry the intended statistics and optional geometry/timing fields.
- Tracks can be materialized and queried as a proper derived dataset.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Track schema, configuration, and cache contract`.

## Non-Goals
- Every map or event-oriented behavior.
- A geometry-first implementation that defeats the scaling goal.
design: Native track-engine epic. Keep the core path columnar and lazy-friendly as long as possible.
notes: Future-self note: whenever a shortcut wants to collect everything eagerly, re-check the scale goals in the v3 plan.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Native segmentation pipeline and Phase 3. Sequencing rationale: once the track contract is explicit, the engine can be implemented without silently defining the dataset shape itself.

title: P3: Implement segmentation boundary detection for gaps, resets, and implausible jumps
description:
## Context
Track building starts by deciding where one movement segment ends and the next begins.

## Planned Work
- Sort positions by vessel and time.
- Detect boundaries from observation gaps, non-monotonic resets, and implausible jumps.
- Keep the boundary logic explicit enough for later tuning and testing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Boundary detection covers the planned segmentation triggers.
- Boundary logic is explicit and testable.
- Track segmentation is not hidden inside opaque aggregation code.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Define track cache keys and invalidation triggers`.

## Non-Goals
- Every future vessel-behavior heuristic.
- Event-level semantics.
design: Segmentation-engine task. Boundary logic should be inspectable because later event detectors depend on it.
notes: Future-self note: when a track split feels surprising, this is the logic future debugging will need to inspect.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Native segmentation pipeline. Sequencing rationale: boundary detection is the first executable step after the track contract is in place.

title: P3: Aggregate track statistics and emit optional geometry/timestamp-offset payloads
description:
## Context
After segmentation, Neptune needs to turn segments into useful track records and map-friendly payloads.

## Planned Work
- Aggregate track-level counts, timing, distance, speed, and bbox fields.
- Optionally emit WKB geometry and per-vertex timestamp offsets.
- Keep the output aligned with the canonical track schema.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Track outputs include the planned summary fields.
- Optional geometry and offsets are available without becoming mandatory in the core path.
- Aggregation output matches the track contract.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Implement segmentation boundary detection for gaps, resets, and implausible jumps`.

## Non-Goals
- Visualization itself.
- Pandas-first geometry handling.
design: Aggregation task for track-level outputs and map-oriented payloads.
notes: Future-self note: optional geometry should stay optional, but when emitted it should still be treated as part of the tracked contract.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Canonical dataset `tracks` and Visualization architecture. Sequencing rationale: aggregate after segmentation so outputs reflect explicit track boundaries.

title: P3: Materialize tracks as a cached derived dataset with read/write integration
description:
## Context
Track logic only becomes reusable across notebooks and workflows once it lands as a proper dataset with cache semantics.

## Planned Work
- Write track outputs using the derived-store and manifest model.
- Read tracks back through the intended dataset access path.
- Tie materialization to the earlier cache-key and invalidation rules.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tracks can be written and read as a first-class derived dataset.
- Track materialization respects cache identity and invalidation rules.
- Phase 4 can depend on track outputs as a stable input.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Aggregate track statistics and emit optional geometry/timestamp-offset payloads`.

## Non-Goals
- Event derivation.
- A one-off export-only track path.
design: Dataset-materialization task for reusable track outputs.
notes: Future-self note: derived datasets become dangerous if they do not inherit the same catalog discipline as canonical partitions.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Derived store and track dataset caching guidance. Sequencing rationale: materialization should follow the completed track engine so the derived output is stable.

title: P3: Geometry bridges, viewport-aware layers, and track/map validation
description:
## Context
This epic exposes the initial map story for positions and tracks while keeping the core path Arrow/columnar-friendly and viewport-aware.

## Why This Matters
A map layer should be a product output of Neptune, not a forced eager conversion step that scales poorly and hides reusable derived products.

## Scope
- Provide optional geometry bridges where they add value.
- Build viewport-aware map-layer products for positions, tracks, trips, and density-ready paths.
- Validate map outputs and track/map integration against fixtures.

## Success Criteria
- Map-layer generation is aligned with the derived track and canonical positions model.
- Optional geometry bridges remain optional.
- Phase 3 ends with reusable map-ready behavior rather than ad hoc conversions.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Native segmentation, aggregation, and track dataset materialization`.

## Non-Goals
- The full event-visualization story.
- Making GeoPandas the core execution engine.
design: Visualization-foundation epic. Keep map preparation close to derived products and far from ad hoc eager conversion sprawl.
notes: Future-self note: if a map feature requires collecting the whole dataset eagerly, treat that as a warning sign rather than a normal cost.
close_reason: All 3 children closed: geometry bridges, viewport-aware layer preparation, and Phase 3 validation fixtures. Epic success criteria met.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Visualization architecture and Phase 3. Sequencing rationale: map foundations should be built on top of real track outputs, not on speculative geometry conversions.

title: P3: Add optional GeoPandas and MovingPandas bridges at the edges
description:
## Context
Neptune should interoperate with geometry-heavy libraries without making them mandatory in the core track path.

## Planned Work
- Define optional conversion helpers for GeoDataFrame and MovingPandas use.
- Keep these bridges separate from the native track derivation path.
- Preserve compatibility with the canonical track output shape.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Optional geometry bridges exist as edge integrations.
- The native track path does not depend on geometry-heavy stacks.
- Bridge outputs align with the track dataset contract.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Materialize tracks as a cached derived dataset with read/write integration`.

## Non-Goals
- Making geometry-heavy libraries mandatory.
- Treating the bridge outputs as the canonical track dataset.
design: Interoperability task at the edge of the core path.
notes: Future-self note: bridges should reduce friction for downstream users, not become the default implementation path.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Optional geometry bridges section. Sequencing rationale: bridge work belongs after the native track output exists.

title: P3: Build viewport-aware positions, tracks, trips, and density-ready layer preparation
description:
## Context
The visualization layer should be fed by map-ready products that can clip, sample, and prepare dense data efficiently.

## Planned Work
- Prepare viewport-aware layer inputs for positions and tracks.
- Support trip playback prerequisites and density-ready layer preparation.
- Keep map-layer preparation reusable for notebook and export flows.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Layer preparation can clip or sample to a viewport where needed.
- Positions, tracks, and trip-style map paths have a reusable preparation layer.
- Density-ready preparation exists without requiring full Phase 4 analytics yet.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Add optional GeoPandas and MovingPandas bridges at the edges`.

## Non-Goals
- Full event visualization.
- A final polished cartography system.
design: Map-preparation task for the initial Neptune visualization surface.
notes: Future-self note: map-ready products are reusable assets; avoid burying the logic inside one display helper.
close_reason: Implemented viewport-aware layer preparation for positions, tracks, trips, and density in viz.py with full test suite (22 tests)

comments.author: David Ten

title: P3: Add track and map validation fixtures for Phase 3 acceptance
description:
## Context
Phase 3 needs fixtures and acceptance coverage that prove both the track path and the map-preparation path work together.

## Planned Work
- Create fixtures for segmentation edge cases, track outputs, and map-preparation behavior.
- Capture Phase 3 acceptance in a repeatable validation story.
- Preserve these fixtures for later event and visualization work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Track and map paths are covered by explicit Phase 3 validation artifacts.
- Map-preparation behavior is testable rather than visual-inspection-only.
- Phase 4 can build on a stable movement and map foundation.

## Dependencies
- `P3: Build viewport-aware positions, tracks, trips, and density-ready layer preparation`.

## Non-Goals

- Event heuristics.

notes: Future-self note: visual features are easiest to regress quietly; fixture them early.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Phase 3 validation expectations. Sequencing rationale: validate after the track and map foundations are both in place.

title: Phase 4: Events and higher-level maritime helpers
description:
## Context
Phase 4 exposes maritime questions as first-class features through event inference, helper APIs, and event-aware presentation.

## Why This Matters
This phase is where Neptune stops being merely a data plumbing system and starts answering common maritime questions directly.

## Scope
- Define event schema/cache behavior and boundary-aware derivation support.
- Implement port calls, EEZ crossings, encounters, loitering, density, and helper APIs such as snapshots and vessel history.
- Add event visualization and event-focused acceptance coverage.

## Success Criteria
- Core event families and helper APIs are exposed through stable accessors.
- Event outputs include confidence and lineage semantics.
- Users can inspect or visualize event results without reconstructing the logic themselves.

## Dependencies
- Phases 2 and 3 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Domain-complete maritime analytics.
- Every possible boundary dataset.
- Streaming event inference beyond the scoped streaming foundation.
design: Higher-level maritime product layer. Keep heuristics explicit and provenance attached to inferred results.
notes: Future-self note: event definitions age poorly when their assumptions are not documented, so record confidence semantics and heuristic boundaries clearly.
close_reason: Phase 4 complete. All 4 child epics closed: event schema/cache/access (5.1), boundary/port-call/EEZ (5.2), encounters/loitering/density/helpers (5.3), viz/regression/docs (5.4). 365 tests passing.

title: P4: Event schema, cache contract, and event access surfaces
description:
## Context
This epic defines what an event is in Neptune, how event caches are keyed, and how event data becomes accessible through the main product surfaces.

## Why This Matters
If event schema, lineage, and access rules are vague, the higher-level maritime helper story will turn into a collection of notebook heuristics rather than a product feature.

## Scope
- Define canonical event fields and cache identity expectations.
- Define event confidence and provenance semantics.
- Expose the main event accessor and CLI/event surface shape.

## Success Criteria
- The event dataset has a stable schema and cache story.
- Event confidence and lineage are explicit rather than implied.
- Users can access event outputs through the planned Neptune and CLI surfaces.

## Dependencies
- Phases 2 and 3 complete.

## Non-Goals
- All event heuristics at once.
- Boundary-specific implementation details before the contract exists.
design: Event-contract epic. Keep inferred-event semantics explicit so later heuristics do not silently redefine the dataset.
notes: Future-self note: event ambiguity compounds quickly; record cache keys and provenance rules before adding more event families.
close_reason: All 3 children closed: events/v1 schema+cache (5.1.1), confidence+provenance semantics (5.1.2), API accessor+CLI (5.1.3). 81 tests, all success criteria met.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Canonical dataset `events`, Python API summary, CLI, and Phase 4. Sequencing rationale: event contracts and access surfaces need to exist before individual event families expand.

title: P4: Define the canonical `events` schema and cache-key rules
description:
## Context
Event outputs need a stable structure before Neptune can cache and query them reliably.

## Planned Work
- Define the core event fields for identity, timing, geometry, source, confidence, and lineage.
- Specify how event caches are keyed and invalidated.
- Keep the contract broad enough for multiple event families without collapsing them together.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The `events` schema is explicit and implementation-ready.
- Cache-key and invalidation expectations are documented.
- Later event detectors share one dataset contract.

## Dependencies
- Phases 2 and 3 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Boundary-specific heuristics.
- A one-off schema per event family.

notes: Future-self note: event caches should be invalidated by the same kinds of truth changes that affect tracks and fusion.

title: P4: Define event confidence, provenance, and inferred-lineage semantics
description:
## Context
Unlike raw positions, many events are inferred and need explicit confidence and lineage handling.

## Planned Work
- Define how inferred events record confidence.
- Preserve enough lineage to tie events back to inputs, heuristics, and source context.
- Keep event provenance compatible with manifest-level reporting and debugging.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Event confidence semantics are explicit.
- Inferred events retain useful lineage.
- Event outputs can be explained rather than treated as opaque detections.

## Dependencies
- `P4: Define the canonical `events` schema and cache-key rules`.

## Non-Goals
- A full explainability UI.
- Treating event confidence as an unstructured note.
design: Confidence-and-lineage task for inferred products.
notes: Future-self note: if event confidence is not explicit, users will project certainty onto heuristic outputs.
close_reason: Defined confidence bands (high/medium/low), classify_confidence(), EventProvenance dataclass with to_token()/parse_provenance() roundtrip, and 16 new tests

title: P4: Boundary integration, port calls, and EEZ crossings
description:
## Context
This epic covers the boundary-aware event families that require port or EEZ context as part of detection and interpretation.

## Why This Matters
Boundary-aware analytics are core maritime questions, but they only remain trustworthy if the boundary source and provenance story are explicit.

## Scope
- Integrate boundary and region datasets into the event pipeline.
- Implement port-call inference.
- Implement EEZ-crossing inference.

## Success Criteria
- Boundary data has an explicit integration path.
- Port-call and EEZ-crossing events are implemented against the shared event contract.
- Boundary provenance is preserved rather than assumed.

## Dependencies
- `P4: Event schema, cache contract, and event access surfaces`.

## Non-Goals
- Every possible boundary dataset.
- A geopolitical truth service.
design: Boundary-aware event epic. Keep upstream boundary provenance visible because geopolitical or port datasets change over time.
notes: Future-self note: a boundary dataset is not neutral metadata; its version and source matter.
close_reason: All 3 children closed: boundary registry+provenance (5.2.1), port-call detector (5.2.2), EEZ-crossing detector (5.2.3). 73 tests, all success criteria met.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: boundary support, EEZ crossings, and port calls. Sequencing rationale: boundary-aware event families should build on the shared event contract, not define their own.

title: P4: Integrate boundary and region datasets with explicit provenance handling
description:
## Context
Port-call and EEZ-crossing detection depend on geometry or region context that Neptune itself does not generate.

## Planned Work
- Define how Neptune loads or references boundary datasets.
- Preserve provenance and version information for boundary sources.
- Make boundary integration reusable across multiple event families.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Boundary integration is explicit and reusable.
- Boundary provenance is preserved.
- Later event detectors can consume the same boundary layer without reimplementing ingestion.

## Dependencies
- `P4: Expose event dataset accessors and CLI/event surface shape`.

## Non-Goals
- A general GIS platform.
- Hiding boundary-source assumptions.
design: Boundary-integration task for Phase 4 event work.
notes: Future-self note: if a boundary source changes, Neptune should be able to explain which event outputs depended on it.

title: P4: Implement port-call inference on top of the shared event contract
description:
## Context
Port calls are one of the clearest higher-level maritime questions Neptune should answer directly.

## Planned Work
- Define port-call detection inputs and heuristics against canonical positions or tracks and boundary data.

- Preserve confidence and provenance for port-call detections.

## Acceptance Criteria

## Non-Goals
- All possible berth-level semantics.
- Unexplained heuristic thresholds.

title: P4: Implement EEZ-crossing inference with boundary-aware provenance
description:
## Context
EEZ crossings are a key geography-aware event family and a strong example of Neptune’s higher-level value.

## Planned Work
- Detect EEZ-crossing events from movement or point data and boundary context.
- Emit crossings through the shared event contract with representative geometry or location context.
- Preserve boundary provenance and event confidence semantics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- EEZ-crossing events are generated through the shared event pipeline.
- Boundary context and provenance remain attached.
- The detector aligns with the event schema and access surfaces.

## Dependencies
- `P4: Implement port-call inference on top of the shared event contract`.

## Non-Goals
- Resolving all geopolitical disputes.
- Assuming one canonical boundary interpretation without provenance.
design: EEZ-crossing detector task. Preserve boundary provenance because boundary choice materially affects outputs.
notes: Future-self note: EEZ outputs are only as defensible as the boundary metadata attached to them.

title: P4: Encounters, loitering, density, and helper APIs
description:
## Context
This epic covers non-boundary event families plus the helper APIs that make Neptune answer common maritime questions directly.

## Why This Matters
Encounters, loitering, density, and snapshot-style helpers are where Neptune starts to feel like a domain product instead of a data-access package.

## Scope
- Implement encounter and loitering detection.
- Implement density-ready analytics and helper APIs such as latest positions, snapshots, and vessel history.
- Keep these outputs aligned with the broader event and provenance model.

## Success Criteria
- Interaction-style events are available through the event pipeline.
- Density and helper outputs are explicit product surfaces, not notebook snippets.
- Derived helper outputs still honor provenance and QC expectations.

## Dependencies
- `P4: Event schema, cache contract, and event access surfaces`.

## Non-Goals
- Every conceivable vessel-behavior detector.
- A complete analytics language.
design: Helper-and-analytics epic. Keep product-facing helpers close to the canonical datasets and event model instead of scattering them across notebooks.
notes: Future-self note: helper APIs become long-term user contracts quickly, so only add the ones that clearly map to the product goals.
close_reason: All 3 children closed: encounter detector (5.3.1), loitering+density (5.3.2), helper APIs (5.3.3). 61 tests across encounters, loitering, density, and helpers. All success criteria met.

comments.author: David Ten

title: P4: Implement encounter inference as a first-class event family
description:
## Context
Encounter detection is a distinctive higher-level question that helps Neptune feel domain-aware rather than row-oriented.

## Planned Work
- Define encounter heuristics and thresholds against canonical positions or tracks.
- Emit encounter events through the shared event contract.
- Preserve confidence, secondary-vessel linkage, and provenance context.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Encounter events are produced through the event pipeline.
- Secondary-vessel context is represented where relevant.
- The detector is explicit enough for future validation and tuning.

## Dependencies
- `P4: Expose event dataset accessors and CLI/event surface shape`.

## Non-Goals
- Every pairwise interaction pattern.
- Hidden heuristics with no tuning story.

notes: Future-self note: encounter logic gets expensive quickly; keep the first version scoped and explicit.

title: P4: Implement loitering detection and density-oriented derived products
description:
## Context
Loitering and density are two different but related ways to highlight spatial behavior that matters to analysts.

## Planned Work
- Implement loitering detection with explicit duration and behavior assumptions.
- Implement density-oriented derived outputs that later map layers can consume.
- Keep both outputs compatible with the broader derived-data and event story.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Loitering events are available as first-class outputs.
- Density products exist in a reusable derived form.
- The implementations preserve the configuration and provenance context needed for future tuning.

## Dependencies
- `P4: Implement encounter inference as a first-class event family`.

## Non-Goals
- Every clustering algorithm or heatmap style.
- Hidden heuristic thresholds.
design: Behavior-and-density task. Preserve explicit assumptions so the outputs remain interpretable.
notes: Future-self note: loitering and density both look obvious in hindsight; document what the detector actually means.

title: P4: Expose latest-position, snapshot, and vessel-history helper APIs
description:
## Context
The v3 plan promises helpers that answer common maritime questions directly without requiring users to recreate them every time.

## Non-Goals

title: P4: Event visualization, regression coverage, and heuristic guardrails
description:
## Context
This epic closes the Phase 4 loop by making events visible, testable, and documented enough to remain trustworthy over time.

## Why This Matters
Heuristic event systems decay quickly unless their outputs, tests, and guardrails are maintained together.

## Scope
- Add event-oriented visualization layers.
- Add event acceptance and regression coverage.
- Document heuristic assumptions and phase-level scope boundaries.

## Success Criteria
- Event layers can be visualized through the Neptune map path.
- Regression coverage exists for the implemented event families.
- Heuristic assumptions and intentional non-goals are recorded where future contributors will see them.

## Dependencies
- `P4: Boundary integration, port calls, and EEZ crossings`.
- `P4: Encounters, loitering, density, and helper APIs`.

## Non-Goals
- A final polished cartography system.
- Pretending the first event heuristics are domain-complete.
design: Experience-and-guardrail epic. Keep outputs visible and heuristics documented so Phase 4 remains maintainable.
notes: Future-self note: event features are easy to demo and easy to overstate; let tests and scope notes keep them honest.
close_reason: All 3 children closed: event viz layer (5.4.1), acceptance tests (5.4.2), heuristic documentation (5.4.3). All success criteria met.

## Context
Phase 4 introduces the most heuristic-heavy part of Neptune so far, which needs visible guardrails for future maintainers and users.

title: Phase 5: Streaming hardening
description:
## Context
Phase 5 makes streaming an operationally credible part of Neptune by defining lifecycle, checkpoints, buffering, compaction, promotion, and sink behavior.

## Why This Matters
Streaming should not be a forever-append demo path; it needs explicit guarantees and failure semantics that match the seriousness of the archival core.

## Scope
- Define `NeptuneStream` and its live-source integration model.
- Implement retry, checkpoint, backpressure, dedup, compaction, sink, and promotion behavior.
- Add health visibility and long-running reliability coverage.

## Success Criteria
- Streaming sessions can restart safely from checkpoints.
- Landing and promotion semantics are observable and testable.
- Parquet and DuckDB sinks support durable long-running workflows.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Fully managed orchestration.
- A broad live-source provider ecosystem.
- Pretending archival and streaming guarantees are identical.
design: Operational streaming foundation. Make failure modes and promotion semantics visible instead of implicit.
notes: Future-self note: streaming complexity hides in retries, buffer pressure, and promotion boundaries, not in the iterator itself.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Streaming architecture, Source adapters, Data sources, Python API summary, Package structure, and Phase 5. Sequencing rationale: streaming needs a mature canonical core before it can be hardened responsibly.

title: P5: NeptuneStream interface, AISStream integration, and restart-safe checkpoints
description:
## Context
This epic establishes the core streaming object, the first live-source integration, and the checkpoint/retry contract that turns streaming into an operational surface.

## Why This Matters
Streaming needs a clear lifecycle and restart story before sink, promotion, and monitoring work can be trustworthy.

## Scope
- Define the `NeptuneStream` async interface.
- Integrate AISStream as the reference live source.
- Define checkpoint, reconnect, and retry semantics for restart-safe operation.

## Success Criteria
- `NeptuneStream` has an explicit interface and lifecycle.
- AISStream is integrated as a real live source rather than a demo-only path.
- Checkpoint and retry behavior are defined clearly enough for later sinks and promotion work.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- A broad live-source ecosystem.
- Production orchestration outside Neptune’s scoped runtime semantics.
design: Streaming-core epic. Keep the operational contract explicit before optimizations or extra providers multiply complexity.
notes: Future-self note: streaming pain usually hides in restart semantics, not in the iterator signature itself.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Streaming architecture and Phase 5. Sequencing rationale: define the stream object, live source, and restart model before adding more operational layers.

title: P5: Define the `NeptuneStream` async interface and lifecycle semantics
description:
## Context
The stream object is the entry point for live ingestion and sink workflows, so its lifecycle needs to be explicit.

## Planned Work
- Define async iteration, sink hookup, and stream lifecycle expectations.
- Separate stream configuration from archival `Neptune` configuration where needed.
- Keep the interface small enough to harden well.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `NeptuneStream` has an explicit interface and lifecycle story.
- Stream setup is distinguishable from archival dataset access.
- Later sink and promotion work has a stable entry point.

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Every future streaming helper at once.
- A fully managed orchestration framework.
design: Core-interface task for live streaming workflows.
notes: Future-self note: if the stream object grows a kitchen-sink API too early, it will be hard to keep operational semantics coherent.
close_reason: Implemented NeptuneStream async interface with context manager lifecycle, async iteration, message ingestion with O(1) rolling dedup, pluggable StreamSink protocol, run_sink batch runner, StreamConfig, StreamStats, and 21 tests

title: P5: Integrate AISStream as the reference live-source adapter
description:
## Context
A streaming architecture needs at least one real live-source path to shape normalization, retry, and monitoring behavior.

## Planned Work
- Implement AISStream connectivity and source-specific handling.
- Normalize incoming live messages into Neptune’s landing shape.
- Keep the adapter aligned with the shared source and provenance model.

## Acceptance Criteria
- AISStream behaves like a real live-source adapter.
- Live messages can be normalized into Neptune’s landing path.
- The integration uses the broader source/provenance model rather than bypassing it.

## Dependencies

## Non-Goals
- A broad streaming-provider matrix.
- Treating live messages as an archival-format special case.

notes: Future-self note: the first live adapter should clarify the streaming model, not create source-specific exceptions everywhere.

title: P5: Define checkpoint, reconnect, and retry semantics for restart-safe streaming
description:
## Context
A live stream only becomes operationally useful when it can reconnect, retry, and resume with explicit state semantics.

## Planned Work
- Define checkpoint storage and resume cursor expectations.
- Define reconnect and retry behavior for transient failures.
- Make restart-safe operation a first-class part of the stream contract.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Checkpoint semantics are explicit.
- Retry/reconnect behavior is documented tightly enough to implement and test.
- Streaming restart behavior is not left implicit.

## Dependencies
- `P5: Integrate AISStream as the reference live-source adapter`.

## Non-Goals
- Perfect exactly-once guarantees beyond the scoped design.
- Silent reconnect behavior with no defined state model.
design: Operational-semantics task for restart-safe live ingestion.
notes: Future-self note: if retries and resumes are not explicit, every downstream sink or promotion path inherits ambiguity.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: checkpointing and reconnect/retry requirements. Sequencing rationale: restart-safe behavior should be explicit before buffer, sink, or promotion work builds on it.

title: P5: Backpressure, rolling dedup, compaction, and health visibility
description:
## Context
This epic covers the operational controls that keep streaming stable during long runs: buffers, rolling dedup, compaction, and visibility into lag or health.

## Why This Matters
Streaming failure modes usually appear under sustained pressure, not in a happy-path iterator loop.

## Scope
- Define bounded buffers and backpressure behavior.
- Define rolling dedup and compaction semantics.
- Expose heartbeat, lag, or health visibility.

## Success Criteria
- Backpressure behavior is explicit rather than incidental.
- Rolling dedup and compaction are modeled as part of the stream path.
- Operators or users can tell whether the stream is healthy or falling behind.

## Dependencies
- `P5: NeptuneStream interface, AISStream integration, and restart-safe checkpoints`.

## Non-Goals
- Full distributed stream processing.
- Infinite in-memory buffering.
design: Streaming-operations epic. Preserve explicit pressure and health semantics so long-running sessions are understandable.
notes: Future-self note: if pressure handling is left implicit, the first production-like run becomes the real design review.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Streaming requirements and Pluggable sinks. Sequencing rationale: operational controls should build directly on the core stream lifecycle and checkpoint model.

title: P5: Define bounded buffers and backpressure behavior for live ingestion
description:
## Context
Long-running streams need explicit answers for what happens when ingestion outpaces processing or sink throughput.

## Planned Work
- Define bounded-buffer behavior and overflow handling.
- Preserve enough observability to tell when pressure is occurring.
- Keep the behavior compatible with restart and sink semantics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Backpressure behavior is explicit and bounded.
- Pressure handling can be explained rather than guessed.
- Later sink work does not assume infinite buffering.

## Dependencies
- `P5: Define checkpoint, reconnect, and retry semantics for restart-safe streaming`.

## Non-Goals
- Infinite queues.
- Silent dropping with no observability.
design: Pressure-control task for operational live ingestion.
notes: Future-self note: the fastest way to ship a deceptive stream system is to leave buffer behavior implicit.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: bounded buffers / backpressure requirement. Sequencing rationale: pressure handling follows the core restart and lifecycle model.

title: P5: Define rolling dedup and compaction behavior for streaming landing data
description:
## Context
Live streams can contain duplicates, stale repetitions, or data that benefits from rolling compaction before promotion.

## Planned Work
- Define the rolling dedup window and scope.
- Define when or how compaction occurs for landed stream data.
- Keep the behavior aligned with provenance and promotion rules.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Rolling dedup semantics are explicit.
- Compaction behavior is part of the stream model rather than an afterthought.
- Promotion work can rely on a defined landed-data shape.

## Dependencies
- `P5: Define bounded buffers and backpressure behavior for live ingestion`.

## Non-Goals
- Archival-style exact dedup across all history.
- Hidden compaction that changes semantics silently.
design: Landing-data hygiene task for streaming workflows.
notes: Future-self note: compaction changes what users can inspect later, so make it explicit and observable.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: rolling dedup / compaction requirement. Sequencing rationale: dedup and compaction follow pressure handling because both shape landed data under long-running conditions.

title: P5: Expose heartbeat, lag, and health visibility for long-running streams
description:
## Context
Users and operators need to know whether the stream is alive, behind, or unhealthy before they trust its outputs.

## Planned Work
- Define heartbeat and lag visibility for running streams.
- Surface health state in a form sinks or operators can inspect.
- Keep monitoring semantics aligned with checkpoint and backpressure behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Heartbeat or lag visibility exists for long-running streams.
- Health state is inspectable rather than inferred indirectly.
- Operational issues are easier to diagnose than with a silent loop.

## Dependencies
- `P5: Define rolling dedup and compaction behavior for streaming landing data`.

## Non-Goals
- A full external monitoring stack.
- Invisible health semantics.
design: Operational-visibility task for streaming runs.
notes: Future-self note: if the stream dies quietly, users will blame everything except the actual visibility gap.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: heartbeat and source-lag monitoring requirement. Sequencing rationale: health visibility should reflect the now-defined lifecycle, pressure, and dedup semantics.

title: P5: Streaming sinks, canonical promotion, and soak validation
description:
## Context
This epic closes the streaming loop by landing data durably, promoting it into canonical partitions, and validating the whole path under long-running conditions.

## Why This Matters
Streaming is only a credible product path when it can land data durably, promote it into the broader Neptune store, and survive sustained operation.

## Scope
- Implement Parquet and DuckDB sinks.
- Define promotion from landing zone to canonical partitions.
- Add soak and reliability validation for the streaming path.

## Success Criteria
- Streaming data can land durably in supported sinks.
- Promotion into canonical storage reuses the same trust guarantees as archival writes.
- Soak-style validation exists for the complete streaming path.

## Dependencies
- `P5: Backpressure, rolling dedup, compaction, and health visibility`.

## Non-Goals
- Full production orchestration.
- Assuming the landing zone is equivalent to committed canonical storage.
design: Sink-and-promotion epic. Preserve the same data-trust discipline in the live path that Phase 1 established for archival data.
notes: Future-self note: promotion is where streaming either becomes part of Neptune or remains an isolated append path.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Pluggable sinks, promotion requirement, and Phase 5 deliverables. Sequencing rationale: durable sinks and promotion should build on the already-defined operational semantics.

title: P5: Implement Parquet and DuckDB sinks for live-stream landing
description:
## Context
The first durable stream sinks should match the broader execution story of Neptune.

## Planned Work
- Implement a Parquet sink for rolling landed data.
- Implement a DuckDB sink for direct SQL-oriented landing workflows.
- Keep both sinks aligned with provenance and restart expectations.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Both Parquet and DuckDB sink paths are explicit.
- Sinks fit the broader Neptune execution model.
- Sink outputs remain compatible with later promotion work.

## Dependencies
- `P5: Expose heartbeat, lag, and health visibility for long-running streams`.

## Non-Goals
- Every future sink type.
- Source-specific sink semantics.
design: Durable-sink task for the first supported landing paths.
notes: Future-self note: a sink that cannot preserve enough provenance will create mystery later during promotion or debugging.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Pluggable sinks and Phase 5 deliverables. Sequencing rationale: durable sinks should arrive after lifecycle, pressure, and visibility semantics are stable.

title: P5: Define and implement promotion from landing data into canonical partitions
description:
## Context
Live landing data should eventually become part of the same canonical store Neptune uses for archival data when the rules allow it.

## Planned Work
- Define promotion rules from landing-zone batches into canonical partitions.
- Reuse schema, manifest, commit, and provenance guarantees from the archival path.
- Keep the distinction between landed and promoted data explicit.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Promotion semantics are explicit and reuse the canonical storage trust model.
- Promoted data can be traced back to its landing batch and live source.
- The stream path participates in Neptune’s broader catalog story.

## Dependencies
- `P5: Implement Parquet and DuckDB sinks for live-stream landing`.

## Non-Goals
- Pretending raw landing files are already canonical.
- Bypassing manifest and commit guarantees for convenience.
design: Canonical-promotion task for integrating live data into the broader platform.
notes: Future-self note: promotion is a contract boundary, not just a file move.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: stream-to-canonical landing pipeline and atomic write principles. Sequencing rationale: promotion follows durable sink behavior so its inputs are well-defined.

title: P5: Add soak and reliability validation for the full streaming path
description:
## Context
Streaming correctness has to survive time, retries, and sustained load rather than only unit-style checks.

## Planned Work
- Add soak-style validation for the stream, sink, and promotion path.
- Capture restart, lag, and reliability expectations in long-running scenarios.
- Preserve scenarios that later release-readiness work can reuse.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The streaming path has explicit long-running validation coverage.
- Restart and reliability expectations are tested.
- Phase 5 can be treated as a hardened milestone rather than a demo path.

## Dependencies
- `P5: Define and implement promotion from landing data into canonical partitions`.

## Non-Goals
- Full production SRE certification.
- One-off manual soak runs with no repeatability.
design: Soak-validation task for the complete streaming milestone.
notes: Future-self note: a stream path that only passes short tests is still largely unproven.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: streaming soak/reliability requirements and Phase 5 deliverables. Sequencing rationale: long-running validation should close out the first complete streaming path.

title: Phase 7: Release readiness
description:
## Context
Phase 7 certifies Neptune as a coherent releasable package through benchmarks, certification harnesses, migration/reproducibility coverage, packaging, and release-candidate checks.

## Why This Matters
Without explicit release-readiness work, the project risks shipping a technically impressive but operationally ambiguous prototype.

## Scope
- Benchmark the core paths.
- Certify adapters, migrations, and reproducibility behavior.
- Harden packaging, documentation, and release-candidate validation.

## Success Criteria
- Benchmark baselines exist and are explainable.
- Migration and rebuild behavior are tested intentionally.
- Packaging and documentation can support a public-facing release candidate.

## Dependencies
- Phases 2 through 6 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Indefinite polish loops.
- Major architecture rewrites during certification.
- Claiming guarantees the package does not yet provide.
design: Certification and release phase. Treat this as validation of the intended product, not a grab bag for late architecture discovery.
notes: Future-self note: release work surfaces debt that earlier phases tolerated; convert it into explicit backlog items rather than undocumented triage.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Key implementation notes, Dependencies, CLI, docs/examples intent, and Phase 7. Sequencing rationale: release readiness should validate the whole intended product surface, not only the archival core.

title: Phase 6: Additional sources and ecosystem polish
description:
## Context
Phase 6 broadens Neptune’s ecosystem value through additional adapters, plugin-friendly extension points, richer source discovery, documentation, packaging polish, and an explicit Norway reassessment.

## Why This Matters
Expanding the ecosystem only helps if the core product shape is already credible; this phase turns that credibility into broader coverage and better adoption ergonomics.

## Scope
- Add GFW, Finland, and AISHub integrations.
- Support external adapter registration and richer source catalog/discovery UX.
- Improve docs, examples, packaging extras, CLI polish, and keep Norway as a deliberate reassessment rather than accidental scope creep.

## Success Criteria
- Additional sources fit the same adapter and provenance model as earlier phases.
- External teams have a documented plugin path.
- Docs and packaging reinforce the product story without weakening correctness or reproducibility expectations.

## Dependencies
- Phase 2 complete; some live-oriented work also depends on Phase 5.

## Non-Goals
- Adding every possible source immediately.
- Treating expansion as a substitute for hardening.
- Committing Norway into the main path without justification.
design: Ecosystem expansion phase. Keep adapter quality discipline and avoid letting documentation harden unstable interfaces prematurely.
notes: Future-self note: source expansion is seductive; do not spend ecosystem capital hiding unresolved core-product weaknesses.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Source adapters, Data sources, CLI, Dependencies, Package structure, and Phase 6. Sequencing rationale: growth should follow proof that the architecture generalizes.

title: P6: GFW, Finland, and AISHub source expansion
description:
## Context
This epic expands Neptune’s source coverage after the core archival, fusion, and initial streaming semantics are established.

## Why This Matters
Additional sources should prove that the adapter model generalizes, not just pad the source list.

## Scope
- Add GFW, Finland, and AISHub adapters or equivalent integrations.
- Preserve the shared adapter, provenance, and QC expectations.
- Use the new sources to confirm the architecture scales across delivery styles and data quality profiles.

## Success Criteria
- The three planned additional sources are represented as explicit backlog work.
- Each source extension respects the shared adapter and provenance model.
- Source expansion strengthens rather than dilutes Neptune’s product shape.

## Dependencies
- Phase 2 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Integrating every imaginable source.
- Relaxing quality discipline for the sake of a larger source list.
design: Source-expansion epic. The goal is breadth with discipline, not breadth by exception.
notes: Future-self note: every new source should make the adapter contract look more reusable, not less.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Data sources, Source adapters, and Phase 6. Sequencing rationale: source expansion follows proof that archival and fusion foundations are real.

title: P6: Add the GFW source integration with explicit capability and lineage handling
description:
## Context
GFW adds a global enrichment-oriented source with its own delivery and capability profile.

## Planned Work
- Define or implement the GFW adapter/integration path.
- Normalize its outputs into Neptune’s shared model where applicable.
- Preserve capability, lineage, and QC expectations explicitly.

## Acceptance Criteria
- GFW is represented through the shared source model.
- Its outputs can be reasoned about through the same provenance and QC posture as other sources.
- The integration strengthens Neptune’s ecosystem story.

## Dependencies
- Phase 2 complete.

## Non-Goals
- A special-case one-off import path.
- Pretending GFW is identical to archival point feeds.
design: Source-integration task for GFW with explicit capability handling.
notes: Future-self note: enrichments and event-like feeds still need the same provenance discipline as point data.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Data sources table and Phase 6 deliverables. Sequencing rationale: GFW broadens the source model once the earlier architecture can absorb it.

title: P6: Add the Finland integration with live or mixed-delivery semantics
description:
## Context
Finland is a regional source that exercises some of Neptune’s live or mixed-delivery capabilities.

## Planned Work
- Define or implement the Finland adapter/integration path.
- Normalize Finland outputs into Neptune’s shared model.
- Preserve any live-oriented capability metadata and provenance semantics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Finland is represented through the shared source model.
- Its live or mixed-delivery traits are explicit in capability metadata.
- The integration composes with the earlier streaming and source-discovery work.

## Dependencies
- Phase 2 complete.

## Non-Goals
- A hidden live-path exception model.
- Ignoring source-specific provenance just because the feed is regional.
design: Source-integration task for a regional live/mixed source.
notes: Future-self note: Finland should clarify how regional live-ish sources fit Neptune, not create a separate product mode.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Data sources table and Phase 6 scope. Sequencing rationale: Finland helps test how the broader source model handles mixed delivery styles.

title: P6: Add the AISHub integration with variable-quality expectations made explicit
description:
## Context
AISHub broadens ecosystem coverage but comes with more variable quality characteristics than the earlier reference sources.

## Planned Work
- Define or implement the AISHub adapter/integration path.
- Normalize community-sourced fields into Neptune’s shared model.
- Make QC and provenance expectations explicit so variability is not hidden.

## Acceptance Criteria
- AISHub is integrated through the shared source path.
- Variable-quality expectations are visible in provenance or QC behavior.
- The integration does not weaken the platform’s trust model.

## Dependencies
- Phase 2 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Pretending a variable-quality source needs no explicit caveats.
- A bespoke one-off QC story disconnected from the main framework.
design: Community-source integration task with explicit QC posture.
notes: Future-self note: high-variance sources are exactly where Neptune’s QC and provenance model needs to stay honest.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Data sources table and Phase 6 deliverables. Sequencing rationale: AISHub is useful precisely because it tests whether the platform can stay explicit about variable-quality inputs.

title: P6: External plugin registration, source discovery UX, and examples
description:
## Context
This epic turns Neptune’s adapter ecosystem into something discoverable and extensible for outside teams while improving the narrative examples that explain the broader source story.

## Why This Matters
A plugin model and source-discovery UX are how Neptune grows beyond built-in sources without turning core maintenance into a bottleneck.

## Scope
- Define external adapter registration and discovery.
- Improve source catalog and CLI/source-discovery UX.
- Add docs, examples, and notebooks that communicate the product shape.

## Success Criteria
- External adapter registration has a documented path.
- Source discovery is available through programmatic and CLI surfaces.
- Examples demonstrate Neptune as a coherent platform rather than as isolated functions.

## Dependencies
- `P6: GFW, Finland, and AISHub source expansion`.
- Phase 5 complete for live-aware discovery and docs.

## Non-Goals
- A marketplace or hosted plugin service.
- Docs that outrun the architecture.
design: Ecosystem-enablement epic. Keep plugins and discovery aligned with the core source model rather than inventing a parallel metadata world.
notes: Future-self note: examples and plugin docs should reinforce the product boundaries, not blur them.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: plugin registration, richer source capability metadata, CLI expansion, and Phase 6 docs/examples. Sequencing rationale: discovery and examples should reflect a source model that already works across multiple source types.

title: P6: Define external adapter registration and plugin loading behavior
description:
## Context
External teams should be able to add sources without patching Neptune core directly.

## Planned Work
- Define plugin registration and discovery hooks.
- Document the external adapter contract and loading behavior.
- Keep plugin loading aligned with the same capability and provenance model as built-in adapters.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Plugin registration has an explicit contract.
- External adapters can be reasoned about through the same source model as built-ins.
- The plugin path is documented enough to implement safely.

## Dependencies
- `P6: Add the AISHub integration with variable-quality expectations made explicit`.
- Phase 5 complete for live-aware compatibility.

## Non-Goals
- A hosted extension ecosystem.
- Bypassing the shared adapter contract for external integrations.
design: Plugin-contract task for post-core ecosystem growth.
notes: Future-self note: plugin loading is only useful if external adapters stay legible inside the same source model.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: plugin registration for external adapters. Sequencing rationale: define plugin loading after multiple built-in sources have proven what the shared model needs to preserve.

title: P6: Improve source catalog and CLI/source-discovery UX
description:
## Context
Once the source ecosystem expands, users need better ways to inspect availability, capabilities, and fit for a given workflow.

## Planned Work
- Expose richer source catalog summaries and capability inspection.
- Improve CLI/source-discovery affordances around availability and capability differences.
- Keep discovery aligned with the earlier source-metadata and fusion model.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Source discovery is richer and more usable than the earlier Phase 2 surfaces.
- Programmatic and CLI discovery share the same underlying metadata.
- Users can inspect source fit before running workflows.

## Dependencies
- `P6: Define external adapter registration and plugin loading behavior`.

## Non-Goals
- A hosted UI.
- A discovery surface disconnected from real capability metadata.
design: Discovery-UX task for the expanded source ecosystem.
notes: Future-self note: discovery should help users make source choices, not just show a longer list.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: richer source capability metadata and CLI/source-discovery UX. Sequencing rationale: discovery should mature alongside plugins and expanded sources.

title: P6: Add docs, examples, and notebooks that demonstrate the broader Neptune platform story
description:
## Context
Expanded source support and plugins only help if users can see how Neptune fits together as one platform.

## Planned Work
- Add narrative examples or notebooks spanning archival, fused, derived, and live-aware workflows.
- Show how source discovery and capability inspection fit into normal use.
- Keep the examples aligned with the current stable API and CLI surfaces.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Examples demonstrate the product story rather than isolated one-liners.
- Docs reflect the actual source, fusion, and helper model.
- Phase 6 leaves future users with a clearer mental model of Neptune.

## Dependencies
- `P6: Improve source catalog and CLI/source-discovery UX`.

## Non-Goals
- Writing docs for features that do not exist yet.
- Treating notebooks as the only documentation surface.
design: Narrative-example task for Neptune’s expanded product surface.
notes: Future-self note: examples are architecture communication tools, not just marketing collateral.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: documentation and examples deliverable. Sequencing rationale: examples should land after the expanded source and discovery story is coherent.

title: P6: Packaging extras, CLI polish, and Norway reassessment
description:
## Context
This epic handles the lower-risk polish work that supports adoption plus the explicit record that Norway remains deferred until justified.

## Why This Matters
Not every useful Phase 6 outcome is a new adapter; packaging and CLI polish matter, and Norway should remain a conscious decision rather than stealth scope creep.

## Scope
- Clean up packaging extras and install ergonomics.
- Improve CLI polish around the broadened platform surface.
- Create an explicit Norway reassessment or deferral record.

## Success Criteria
- Packaging extras and install story are clearer.
- CLI polish keeps pace with the broadened source and helper surface.
- Norway remains visible as an intentional deferred decision instead of disappearing.

## Dependencies
- Phase 2 complete.

## Non-Goals
- Secretly expanding Norway into full scope.
- Treating packaging or CLI polish as a substitute for correctness.
design: Polish-and-deferral epic. Keep this work supportive of the product rather than a distraction from it.
notes: Future-self note: a good deferral record is a gift to whoever reopens the question later.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: packaging extras cleanup, CLI expansion, and Norway reassessment guidance in Phase 6. Sequencing rationale: polish and explicit deferral should accompany ecosystem growth without hijacking it.

title: P6: Clean up packaging extras and optional-install ergonomics
description:
## Context
As Neptune grows, its optional install story needs to stay legible.

## Planned Work
- Review packaging extras against the actual dependency groups.
- Clarify install ergonomics for core, geometry, and streaming-adjacent workflows.
- Keep packaging aligned with the architecture rather than with historical accidents.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Packaging extras are clearer and better aligned with subsystem boundaries.
- Users can reason about what to install for their workflow.
- Optional-install ergonomics support the product story rather than undermining it.

## Dependencies
- Phase 2 complete.

## Non-Goals
- A full packaging pipeline redesign.
- Treating packaging polish as a replacement for docs.
design: Packaging-polish task aligned with the earlier dependency-boundary work.
notes: Future-self note: packaging confusion often looks like architecture confusion from the user side.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: dependencies and packaging extras cleanup guidance. Sequencing rationale: extras cleanup should follow enough platform growth to expose real friction.

title: P6: Polish CLI affordances across the expanded Neptune surface
description:
## Context
The CLI should remain coherent as Neptune gains more sources, helpers, and inspection surfaces.

## Planned Work
- Review command naming and consistency across source, fusion, and helper areas.
- Smooth obvious friction in the existing CLI flows without inventing a parallel interface.
- Keep the CLI aligned with the expanded docs and discovery story.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The CLI remains coherent as Neptune’s surface grows.
- Expanded source and helper features feel like part of one interface.
- CLI polish is grounded in the actual platform shape.

## Dependencies
- `P6: Clean up packaging extras and optional-install ergonomics`.

## Non-Goals
- A brand-new CLI design from scratch.
- Breaking earlier users just to pursue novelty.
design: CLI-polish task that keeps the command surface coherent as scope broadens.
notes: Future-self note: polish should remove friction, not rewrite the mental model users already learned.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: CLI expansion and ecosystem polish goals. Sequencing rationale: CLI polish should follow real expanded usage paths and docs.

title: P6: Record the Norway reassessment, deferral rationale, and criteria for reopening it
description:
## Context
The plan intentionally kept Norway visible but deferred rather than silently dropping it or accidentally pulling it into scope.

## Planned Work
- Capture why Norway is deferred in the current plan.
- Record what technical or product conditions would justify reopening the decision.
- Keep the deferral attached to the Phase 6 backlog rather than letting it vanish into oral history.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Norway is explicitly documented as deferred rather than forgotten.
- Reopen criteria are visible to future maintainers.
- Phase 6 closes with an intentional record of this scope choice.

## Dependencies
- `P6: Polish CLI affordances across the expanded Neptune surface`.

## Non-Goals
- Implementing the Norway adapter now.
- Treating deferral as a silent omission.
design: Decision-record task for explicit scope management.
notes: Future-self note: a deferred idea is far easier to revisit when its original reasoning is preserved.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Origin: Norway reassessment assumption in the implementation plan. Sequencing rationale: capture the deferral explicitly once the main Phase 6 work is in place.
